\text{BEP}_{\text{units}} &= \frac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{SP} - \text{VC}} \\
&= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{50 - 30} \\
&= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{20} \\
&= 30{,}000 \text{ units per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate BEP in ₹ Value**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEP}_{₹} &= \text{BEP}_{\text{units}} \times \text{SP} \\
&= 30{,}000 \times 50 \\
&= ₹ 15{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Comment on Viability**

The firm is targeting ₹ 10,00,000 in sales — **below** the BEP of ₹ 15,00,000. This signals **non-viability** under current assumptions and prompts revision of cost structure or pricing.

This kind of **quantitative justification** is exactly what elevates a project report from "average" to "KTU Board Examiner Gold Standard."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture — Contents of a Business Plan (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Cover Page and Title Block"]
    nodeB["Acknowledgement and Table of Contents"]
    nodeC["Executive Summary"]
    nodeD["Chapter 1 Introduction and Promoter Profile"]
    nodeE["Chapter 2 Industry and Market Analysis"]
    nodeF["Chapter 3 Product and Service Description"]
    nodeG["Chapter 4 Marketing Plan 4Ps 7Ps"]
    nodeH["Chapter 5 Operational Plan"]
    nodeI["Chapter 6 Organizational and Legal Structure"]
    nodeJ["Chapter 7 Financial Plan Projections BEP DSCR"]
    nodeK["Chapter 8 Risk Analysis and Mitigation"]
    nodeL["Chapter 9 SWOT Analysis and Conclusion"]
    nodeM["Appendices and Bibliography"]

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG --> nodeH --> nodeI --> nodeJ --> nodeK --> nodeL --> nodeM
```

## 4.2 Formulation Pipeline Flow (Mermaid Process Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Stage 1 Idea Generation"]
    stepB["Stage 2 Idea Screening"]
    stepC["Stage 3 Feasibility Study 3D"]
    stepD["Stage 4 Detailed Research"]
    stepE["Stage 5 Drafting Sections"]
    stepF["Stage 6 Validation and Review"]
    stepG["Stage 7 Final Submission and Pitch"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG
```

## 4.3 3D Feasibility Sub-Diagram (Mermaid Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepX["Feasibility Study"]
    stepY["Market Feasibility"]
    stepZ["Technical Feasibility"]
    stepW["Financial Feasibility"]

    stepX --> stepY
    stepX --> stepZ
    stepX --> stepW

    stepY --> stepY1["Demand Analysis and TAM SAM SOM"]
    stepY --> stepY2["Competitor Mapping"]

    stepZ --> stepZ1["Technology Availability"]
    stepZ --> stepZ2["Raw Material and Manpower"]

    stepW --> stepW1["Cost Projections"]
    stepW --> stepW2["BEP IRR DSCR Calculations"]
```

## 4.4 Financial Plan Inter-Relationship Map (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    finStart["Financial Plan Inputs"]
    finA["Total Project Cost"]
    finB["Means of Finance"]
    finC["Revenue Projections"]
    finD["Cost Projections"]
    finE["P and L Statement"]
    finF["Balance Sheet"]
    finG["Cash Flow Statement"]
    finH["Break Even Chart"]
    finI["DSCR and Loan Repayment"]
    finJ["Viability Decision"]

    finStart --> finA
    finStart --> finB
    finStart --> finC
    finStart --> finD
    finC --> finE
    finD --> finE
    finE --> finF
    finE --> finG
    finE --> finH
    finB --> finI
    finE --> finI
    finE --> finJ
    finH --> finJ
    finI --> finJ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Students:** When asked a 14-mark question on "Explain the contents of a business plan," convert your answer into a **tree diagram first**, then elaborate each node. Visual answers score higher than pure textual answers in KTU board valuations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define a Business Plan. Mention any four key contents of a project report.

**Model Answer:**

A **Business Plan** is a formal written document prepared by an entrepreneur that comprehensively describes the business idea, market opportunity, operational strategy, organizational structure, and financial projections of a proposed venture. It serves as a blueprint for execution and as a fundraising tool.

**Four Key Contents:**
1. **Executive Summary** — Snapshot of the entire plan.
2. **Marketing Plan** — 4Ps/7Ps strategy.
3. **Operational Plan** — Production process and technology.
4. **Financial Plan** — Cost, means of finance, projected P&L, BEP, DSCR.

*(Any 4 valid contents accepted. Word count: 80–100 words.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** What is meant by feasibility study? List its three types.

**Model Answer:**

A **Feasibility Study** is a systematic evaluation conducted before launching a venture to determine whether the proposed business idea is practically viable in the real world. It assesses the strengths, weaknesses, and profitability potential of the idea.

**Three Types:**
1. **Market Feasibility** — Demand, competition, target customer analysis.
2. **Technical Feasibility** — Technology, raw materials, manpower availability.
3. **Financial Feasibility** — Cost, returns, BEP, IRR, DSCR.

*(Definition + 3 types = 3 marks complete.)*

---

## 5.2 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**"Explain in detail the various contents of a project report. Discuss the formulation process step-by-step."**

**Distribution:** Part (a) 7 marks — Contents of project report. Part (b) 7 marks — Formulation process.

#### **Part (a) Model Answer — Contents of a Project Report (7 marks):**

The contents of a project report are systematically organized into **three layers**:

**Layer 1: Preliminary Pages**
- **Cover Page** — Project title, promoter name, address, date.
- **Acknowledgement** — Gratitude to guides, institution.
- **Table of Contents** — Chapter-wise index with page numbers.
- **Executive Summary** — Concise 1–2 page overview of the entire plan.

**Layer 2: Main Body Chapters**
1. **Introduction & Objectives** — Background, problem statement, vision, mission.
2. **Promoter/Entrepreneur Profile** — Educational qualifications, work experience, achievements.
3. **Industry & Market Analysis** — Industry overview, market size, growth trends, SWOT, Porter's analysis.
4. **Product/Service Description** — Features, USP, life cycle stage.
5. **Marketing Plan** — Product, Price, Place, Promotion strategies.
6. **Operational Plan** — Location, plant layout, production process, technology, capacity utilization.
7. **Organizational Plan** — Legal form (Proprietorship/Partnership/LLP/Pvt. Ltd.), hierarchy, manpower.
8. **Financial Plan** — Cost of project, means of finance, projected P&L, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow, BEP, IRR, DSCR.
9. **Risk Analysis & Mitigation** — Identification of internal/external risks and contingency plans.
10. **Legal & Statutory Requirements** — Licenses, registrations, GST, MSME, pollution clearances.

**Layer 3: Closing Pages**
- **SWOT Analysis Summary**
- **Conclusion**
- **Appendices** — Quotations, CVs, brochures.
- **Bibliography** — References.

*(7 marks: 1 for layering logic + 4 marks for listing 10+ contents with one-line explanations + 2 marks for proper sequencing and presentation.)*

#### **Part (b) Model Answer — Formulation Process (7 marks):**

The formulation of a business plan follows a **7-stage sequential pipeline**:

**Stage 1: Idea Generation** — Brainstorming, mind mapping, SCAMPER techniques to generate multiple ideas.

**Stage 2: Idea Screening** — Apply weighted scoring matrix to shortlist the best idea based on personal fit and market potential.

**Stage 3: Feasibility Study** — Conduct 3D analysis (Market, Technical, Financial) to confirm viability.

**Stage 4: Detailed Research** — Collect primary data (surveys, interviews) and secondary data (industry reports, government databases).

**Stage 5: Drafting the Plan** — Write each chapter sequentially; Executive Summary kept for last.

**Stage 6: Validation & Review** — Internal review by guides + external review by bankers/experts.

**Stage 7: Finalization & Submission** — Incorporate feedback, finalize formatting, submit to funding agency or incubator.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per stage with detailed elaboration + 0 marks for skipping any stage.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — *Internal Alternative*

**"Discuss the significance of a business plan. Explain the 3D feasibility study and the Break-Even Analysis in detail."**

**Distribution:** Part (a) 7 marks — Significance of business plan. Part (b) 7 marks — 3D Feasibility + BEP.

#### **Part (a) Model Answer — Significance of Business Plan (7 marks):**

1. **Clarity of Vision** — Translates abstract idea into concrete measurable goals.
2. **Fundraising Instrument** — Mandatory for bank loans, VC funding, and government schemes (PMEGP, MUDRA).
3. **Risk Mitigation** — Identifies potential pitfalls before capital commitment.
4. **Strategic Roadmap** — Provides a phased execution path with milestones.
5. **Performance Benchmark** — Allows actual vs. planned comparison post-launch.
6. **Team Alignment Tool** — Unifies founders, employees, and advisors around shared objectives.
7. **Regulatory Compliance** — Documents legal, tax, and statutory adherence.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per point × 7 = 7.)*

#### **Part (b) Model Answer — 3D Feasibility + BEP (7 marks):**

**3D Feasibility Study** evaluates the venture on three dimensions:

1. **Market Feasibility** — Assesses demand, competition, target segments using TAM-SAM-SOM analysis.
2. **Technical Feasibility** — Confirms technology availability, raw material supply, manpower skills, plant capacity.
3. **Financial Feasibility** — Verifies profitability via BEP, IRR, DSCR, ROI projections.

**Break-Even Analysis (BEP):**
The Break-Even Point is the sales volume at which **Total Revenue = Total Cost** (zero profit, zero loss).

$$
\text{BEP in Units} = \frac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Selling Price} - \text{Variable Cost per unit}}
$$

$$
\text{BEP in Rupees} = \frac{\text{Fixed Cost} \times \text{Selling Price}}{\text{Selling Price} - \text{Variable Cost per unit}}
$$

**Numerical Example:**
If Fixed Cost = ₹ 6,00,000, SP = ₹ 50, VC = ₹ 30:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BEP units} &= \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{50 - 30} = 30{,}000 \text{ bags} \\
\text{BEP in ₹} &= 30{,}000 \times 50 = ₹ 15{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

The firm must sell **30,000 bags** or achieve **₹ 15,00,000** in revenue to break even. Lower BEP indicates lower risk.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for 3D Feasibility explanation + 2 marks for BEP formula + 2 marks for numerical working.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **❌ Random ordering of contents** — Always list project report contents in the **logical document sequence**, not as a random checklist. Examiners penalize sequencing errors up to 2 marks.
> 2. **❌ Confusing Business Plan with Feasibility Study** — They are NOT the same. Feasibility Study is a *Stage 3 input* into the Business Plan; the plan itself is the final output document.
> 3. **❌ Skipping Financial Plan components** — Always mention **BEP, IRR, DSCR, ROI, Pay-Back Period** explicitly. Generic terms like "financial projections" fetch zero marks.
> 4. **❌ Writing Executive Summary first** — Executive Summary is the **FIRST page** but written **LAST**. This is a classic Board trick question.
> 5. **❌ Not mentioning "Means of Finance"** — Every project report MUST show the *equity-debt ratio*. Promoters' contribution must be at least **10–25%** for most government schemes.
> 6. **❌ Missing SWOT in Marketing/Industry chapter** — Examiners expect SWOT as a sub-component of Industry Analysis.
> 7. **❌ Using "Project" and "Business" interchangeably** — A *project* is a time-bound investment proposal; a *business* is an ongoing commercial entity. The plan covers both.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 High-Yield Revision Points — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition of Business Plan** — A written document describing past, present, and future operations of a proposed venture.
- ✅ **Three Layers of Contents** — Preliminary Pages → Main Body Chapters → Closing Pages.
- ✅ **17 Standard Contents** — Cover Page → Acknowledgement → TOC → Executive Summary → Introduction → Promoter Profile → Industry Analysis → Product Description → Marketing Plan → Operational Plan → Organizational Plan → Financial Plan → Risk Analysis → Legal Requirements → SWOT → Conclusion → Appendices → Bibliography.
- ✅ **Formulation = 7 Stages** — Idea → Screening → Feasibility → Research → Drafting → Validation → Submission.
- ✅ **3D Feasibility** — Market, Technical, Financial.
- ✅ **BEP Formula** — $\dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{SP} - \text{VC}}$ (units); $\dfrac{\text{FC} \times \text{SP}}{\text{SP} - \text{VC}}$ (₹).
- ✅ **DSCR Formula** — $\dfrac{\text{Net Profit + Depreciation + Interest}}{\text{Principal + Interest}}$ (must be $\geq 1.5$).
- ✅ **Pay-Back Period** — $\dfrac{\text{Total Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$.
- ✅ **Executive Summary** is written LAST but placed FIRST.
- ✅ **Promoter's Contribution** in Means of Finance is typically **10–25%** of total project cost.
- ✅ **SWOT** stands for **Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats**.
- ✅ **Marketing Mix = 4Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion)** — extended to **7Ps (People, Process, Physical Evidence)** for services.
- ✅ **Project Report ≠ Business Plan ≠ Feasibility Report** — they are related but distinct documents.
- ✅ **TAM-SAM-SOM** — Total Available Market → Serviceable Available Market → Serviceable Obtainable Market.
- ✅ **KTU Bloom's Tag** — This topic tests **Analyze (L4) + Evaluate (L5) + Create (L6)** cognitive levels.
- ✅ **Common Pitfall** — Always end your answer with a concluding statement or viability recommendation; never abruptly stop.

---

*📌 End of Topic Notes — Contents and Formulation | Module 3 | OEEVT614 — Entrepreneurship*
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) | KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of Project Formulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT614 - ENTREPRENEURSHIP
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Elements of Project Formulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Project Formulation** is the systematic, analytical, and structured process of converting a business idea or an identified opportunity into a well-defined, technically feasible, financially viable, and socially acceptable investment proposal. It is the second critical phase of the project cycle (after Project Identification and before Project Appraisal and Implementation) and serves as the **conceptual and quantitative bridge** between a raw entrepreneurial idea and a bankable Detailed Project Report (DPR).

According to the standard KTU 2024 Scheme OEEVT614 syllabus framework, project formulation integrates the outputs of opportunity scanning, feasibility screening, and pre-investment appraisal into a coherent, decision-ready document.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *Project formulation is the process of preparing a detailed investment proposal by systematically analysing the market, technical, financial, managerial, and socio-environmental dimensions of a proposed business venture, culminating in a Detailed Project Report (DPR) suitable for appraisal, financing, and execution.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint" View

Imagine you have an idea to build a two-storey house on a 10-cent plot in Kerala. You would **not** start digging the foundation on day one. Instead, you would:

1. **Discuss the idea** with family (this is *Project Identification*).
2. **Check whether the plot allows a two-storey building**, whether there is road access, water, electricity (this is *Pre-Feasibility*).
3. **Hire an architect** to draw detailed plans, an engineer to check the soil, a structural consultant, and a financial planner to estimate the loan EMI (this is *Project Formulation*).
4. **Submit the plan to the municipal corporation** for approval (this is *Project Appraisal*).
5. **Start construction** (this is *Project Implementation*).

**Project Formulation is the "architect's blueprint" stage of entrepreneurship.** Just as a building cannot rise without structural drawings, a business cannot attract capital, regulatory clearances, or stakeholder confidence without a properly formulated project document.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** In KTU valuation, the term *Project Formulation* is often confused with *Project Identification* or *Project Feasibility*. Remember the hierarchy:
> **Identification** (What idea?) → **Formulation** (How to convert into a proposal?) → **Appraisal** (Is it worth funding?) → **Implementation** (Execute it).

---

### 1.3 The Six Pillars (Core Elements) of Project Formulation

The KTU 2024 Scheme categorises the elements of project formulation into **six inter-locking analytical pillars**:

| # | Element | Core Question Answered | Output |
|---|---------|------------------------|--------|
| 1 | **Market Analysis** | *Who will buy, how much, at what price?* | Demand forecast, market share |
| 2 | **Technical Analysis** | *How will it be produced?* | Plant layout, technology, capacity |
| 3 | **Financial Analysis** | *Is it profitable?* | P&L, cash flow, NPV, IRR |
| 4 | **Managerial / Organisational Analysis** | *Who will run it?* | Org structure, HR plan |
| 5 | **Socio-Environmental Analysis** | *Is it legally and ethically acceptable?* | EIA, CSR, compliance |
| 6 | **Detailed Project Report (DPR)** | *Is the proposal complete?* | Bankable document |

These six pillars, along with **Project Appraisal**, constitute the deliverable structure of Module 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry/Board Benchmark:** A professionally formulated DPR is typically **80–150 pages**, follows the **UNIDO (United Nations Industrial Development Organization)** or **PMP (Project Management Professional)** framework, and is the single most examined deliverable in KTU 14-mark questions on this module.

---

### 1.4 Why Project Formulation Matters in Entrepreneurship

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Non-Negotiable Justifications (Use as Opening Statement in 14-mark answers):**
> 1. **Risk Mitigation:** Approximately **70% of Indian MSMEs fail within 5 years** (RBI/MSME Ministry data) primarily due to poor project formulation rather than weak ideas.
> 2. **Capital Mobilisation:** Banks, SIDBI, NABARD, and venture capitalists **will not release a single rupee** without a properly formulated DPR.
> 3. **Strategic Clarity:** Formulation forces the entrepreneur to confront hidden assumptions about customers, costs, and competition *before* irreversible capital commitment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sequential Logic of Project Formulation

The formulation process follows a **nine-step sequential logic** (often tested as a 7-mark sub-question in KTU ESE):

**Step 1 — Project Idea Refinement**
The raw idea (e.g., "start a cloud kitchen in Kochi") is refined using **SWOT analysis**, **gap analysis**, or **design thinking** to convert it into a focused product-market proposition.

**Step 2 — Preliminary Screening**
A quick filter is applied using the **UNIDO checklist** (Objective, Market, Technology, Finance, Organisation) to drop clearly unviable ideas *before* spending on detailed study.

**Step 3 — Pre-Feasibility Study**
A 10–20 page rapid assessment that estimates investment ranges, rough demand, and technology alternatives.

**Step 4 — Market Analysis (Detailed)**
- **Demand Forecasting** (time-series, regression, end-use method)
- **Supply Analysis** (existing competitors, capacity utilisation)
- **Marketing Mix** (4Ps: Product, Price, Place, Promotion)
- **Market Share Capture** (e.g., 5% of Kerala cloud-kitchen market)

**Step 5 — Technical Analysis**
- **Location Selection** (factor-rating method, break-even location)
- **Plant Capacity** (installed vs. utilised capacity)
- **Technology Choice** (manual / semi-automatic / automatic)
- **Input-Output Analysis** (raw materials, utilities, manpower)
- **Project Schedule** (CPM / PERT network, Gantt chart)

**Step 6 — Financial Analysis**
This is the **most heavily tested element in KTU**. It contains:
- **Cost of Project** (fixed + working capital)
- **Means of Finance** (debt-equity ratio, DSCR)
- **Profitability Projections** (P&L for 5–10 years)
- **Cash Flow Statement** (year-wise inflows & outflows)
- **Investment Evaluation Criteria** — see Formula Sheet below

**Step 7 — Managerial / Organisational Plan**
- **Ownership structure** (proprietorship / partnership / LLP / Pvt. Ltd.)
- **Organisation chart** (Board, MD, functional heads)
- **Manpower plan** (skill, number, cost)

**Step 8 — Socio-Environmental Analysis**
- **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** under the **EIA Notification, 2006**
- **CSR obligations** under **Section 135, Companies Act 2013**
- **Employment generation** (direct + indirect)
- **Statutory clearances** (Pollution Control Board, Factory Inspectorate, FSSAI for food ventures)

**Step 9 — Detailed Project Report (DPR) Compilation**
The final, integrated document combining all eight previous steps.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Financial Evaluation Criteria

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD:** The following seven formulas account for **~60% of marks** in any KTU 14-mark question involving financial analysis. Memorise the formula, units, decision rule, and at least one numeric example for each.

| # | Criterion | Formula | Decision Rule | KTU Frequency |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Payback Period (PBP)** | $PBP = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | Accept if PBP $\leq$ Target period | ★★★★ |
| 2 | **Discounted Payback Period (DPBP)** | $DPBP = \min \{t \mid \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} \geq I_0\}$ | Accept if DPBP $\leq$ Target | ★★★ |
| 3 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | Accept if $NPV \geq 0$ | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | Accept if $IRR \geq r$ (cost of capital) | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** | $BCR = \frac{\sum \frac{B_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | Accept if $BCR \geq 1$ | ★★★★ |
| 6 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI = \frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$ | Accept if $ROI \geq$ Hurdle rate | ★★★ |
| 7 | **Debt-Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR)** | $DSCR = \frac{\text{Net Cash Flow before Debt Service}}{\text{Annual Debt Service}}$ | Accept if $DSCR \geq 1.5$ (bank norm) | ★★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation convention used above:**
> - $CF_t$ = Cash flow in year $t$
> - $I_0$ = Initial investment
> - $r$ = Discount rate / cost of capital
> - $B_t$, $C_t$ = Benefits and costs in year $t$
> - The vertical bar symbol has been intentionally avoided inside table cells to prevent markdown parser breakage.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of Project Formulation

| Domain | How Formulation is Used |
|--------|-------------------------|
| **Startup Funding** | Pitch decks, VC term sheets, and SAFE notes are condensed versions of a DPR |
| **Bank Loans (KSFE, SBI, Kerala Bank)** | DPR is mandatory for term-loan sanction above ₹10 lakh |
| **Government Subsidies (PMEGP, KVIC)** | DPR determines subsidy eligibility under MUDRA / Stand-Up India |
| **Corporate Strategy** | Capex approval in large firms follows the same six-pillar logic |
| **Public-Private Partnerships (PPP)** | DPRs form the basis of bid documents in NHAI / KIIFB projects |

---

### 2.4 The Detailed Project Report (DPR) — Standard Structure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Standard DPR Table of Contents (use this verbatim in 14-mark "DPR contents" questions):**
>
> 1. **Executive Summary** (1 page)
> 2. **Project Profile & Promoter Details**
> 3. **Industry & Market Analysis** (with demand-supply gap)
> 4. **Technical Plan** (capacity, technology, plant layout)
> 5. **Location & Infrastructure**
> 6. **Project Cost & Means of Finance**
> 7. **Financial Projections** (P&L, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow for 5–10 years)
> 8. **Profitability & Sensitivity Analysis**
> 9. **Organisation & HR Plan**
> 10. **Implementation Schedule** (CPM/PERT)
> 11. **Statutory & Environmental Compliances**
> 12. **Risk Analysis & Mitigation**
> 13. **Annexures** (quotations, technical drawings, certificates)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — NPV, IRR, BCR, PBP Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Board-pattern numerical):**
> A Kerala-based entrepreneur proposes to set up a ready-mix concrete (RMC) plant in Ernakulam.
> - **Initial Investment** $I_0$ = ₹ **1,00,00,000** (₹ 1 crore)
> - **Project Life** $n$ = **5 years**
> - **Annual Cash Inflow (after tax)** $CF_t$ = ₹ **32,00,000** for years 1 to 5
> - **Salvage Value** at year 5 = ₹ **10,00,000**
> - **Discount Rate (Cost of Capital)** $r$ = **12% p.a.**
>
> Evaluate the project using NPV, IRR, BCR, and Payback Period.

---

#### Step A — Payback Period (PBP)

PBP does not consider salvage value or time value of money, so we use the simple formula.

$$
PBP = \frac{I_0}{CF_t} = \frac{1{,}00{,}00{,}000}{32{,}00{,}000}
$$

**Calculation logic:** Divide ₹1,00,00,000 by ₹32,00,000 to find the number of years required to recover the initial outlay.

$$
PBP = 3.125 \text{ years} \approx 3 \text{ years and } 1.5 \text{ months}
$$

**Interpretation:** The project recovers its initial cost in 3.13 years, which is well within the **5-year industry benchmark** for RMC plants in Kerala.

---

#### Step B — Net Present Value (NPV) Calculation

**Step B.1 — Compute the present value of the annuity of cash flows (years 1–5).**

The present value annuity factor at 12% for 5 years is given by:

$$
PVIFA(r,n) = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}
$$

Substituting $r = 0.12$ and $n = 5$:

$$
PVIFA(12\%, 5) = \frac{1 - (1.12)^{-5}}{0.12}
$$

**Intermediate calculation logic:** First compute $(1.12)^5$ in order to obtain the reciprocal.

$$
(1.12)^5 = 1.7623 \text{ (rounded to four decimals)}
$$

$$
(1.12)^{-5} = \frac{1}{1.7623} = 0.5674
$$

$$
PVIFA(12\%, 5) = \frac{1 - 0.5674}{0.12} = \frac{0.4326}{0.12} = 3.6048
$$

**Step B.2 — Compute the present value of the salvage value (year 5).**

$$
PV(\text{Salvage}) = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^5} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{1.7623} = 5{,}67{,}427
$$

**Conversion logic:** A salvage realised at the end of year 5 is discounted 5 times, so the denominator is $(1.12)^5 = 1.7623$.

**Step B.3 — Compute the present value of the total inflows.**

$$
PV(\text{Inflows}) = (32{,}00{,}000 \times 3.6048) + 5{,}67{,}427
$$

$$
PV(\text{Inflows}) = 1{,}15{,}35{,}360 + 5{,}67{,}427 = 1{,}21{,}02{,}787
$$

**Step B.4 — Compute NPV.**

$$
NPV = PV(\text{Inflows}) - I_0
$$

$$
NPV = 1{,}21{,}02{,}787 - 1{,}00{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{NPV = +\,21{,}02{,}787 \text{ (positive)}}
$$

**Decision Rule:** Since $NPV \geq 0$, the project is **financially acceptable** and will add **₹ 21.03 lakh** in present-value wealth to the entrepreneur.

---

#### Step C — Internal Rate of Return (IRR) — Trial & Error Method

We must find the rate $r$ such that:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{5} \frac{32{,}00{,}000}{(1+r)^t} + \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{(1+r)^5} - 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 = 0
$$

**Step C.1 — Try r = 18%.**

$$
PVIFA(18\%, 5) = \frac{1 - (1.18)^{-5}}{0.18} = \frac{1 - 0.4371}{0.18} = 3.1272
$$

$$
PV(\text{Inflows at 18\%}) = (32{,}00{,}000 \times 3.1272) + \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{1.18^5}
$$

$$
= 1{,}00{,}07{,}040 + \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{2.2878}
$$

$$
= 1{,}00{,}07{,}040 + 4{,}37{,}109 = 1{,}04{,}44{,}149
$$

$$
NPV_{18\%} = 1{,}04{,}44{,}149 - 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 = +4{,}44{,}149 \text{ (positive)}
$$

**Step C.2 — Try r = 20%.**

$$
PVIFA(20\%, 5) = \frac{1 - (1.20)^{-5}}{0.20} = \frac{1 - 0.4019}{0.20} = 2.9906
$$

$$
PV(\text{Inflows at 20\%}) = (32{,}00{,}000 \times 2.9906) + \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{1.20^5}
$$

$$
= 95{,}69{,}920 + \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{2.4883}
$$

$$
= 95{,}69{,}920 + 4{,}01{,}881 = 99{,}71{,}801
$$

$$
NPV_{20\%} = 99{,}71{,}801 - 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 = -28{,}199 \text{ (negative)}
$$

**Step C.3 — Linear Interpolation.**

Since NPV changes sign between 18% (+) and 20% (-), IRR lies in this range. The interpolation formula is:

$$
IRR = r_L + \frac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (r_H - r_L)
$$

**Conversion logic:** $r_L$ is the lower rate (18%) whose NPV is positive, $r_H$ is the higher rate (20%) whose NPV is negative. The weight is the ratio of the positive NPV to the total NPV span.

$$
IRR = 18\% + \frac{4{,}44{,}149}{4{,}44{,}149 - (-28{,}199)} \times (20\% - 18\%)
$$

$$
IRR = 18\% + \frac{4{,}44{,}149}{4{,}72{,}348} \times 2\%
$$

$$
IRR = 18\% + 0.9404 \times 2\% = 18\% + 1.88\%
$$

$$
\boxed{IRR \approx 19.88\%}
$$

**Decision Rule:** Since $IRR \approx 19.88\% > r = 12\%$, the project is **financially acceptable** with a comfortable safety margin of ~8 percentage points.

---

#### Step D — Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) at 12%

$$
BCR = \frac{PV(\text{Inflows})}{PV(\text{Outflows})} = \frac{1{,}21{,}02{,}787}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{BCR = 1.21}
$$

**Decision Rule:** Since $BCR = 1.21 \geq 1$, the project is acceptable. The project generates **₹ 1.21 of present-value benefit for every ₹ 1.00 of present-value cost**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Criterion | Value Computed | Decision Rule | Decision | Sensitivity |
|-----------|---------------:|---------------|----------|-------------|
| PBP | 3.13 years | $\leq$ 5 years | **Accept** | Low |
| NPV | +₹ 21,02,787 | $\geq$ 0 | **Accept** | Medium |
| IRR | 19.88% | $\geq$ 12% | **Accept** | Medium |
| BCR | 1.21 | $\geq$ 1 | **Accept** | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Inference:** All four criteria unanimously recommend acceptance. In a KTU answer, this is the **gold-standard conclusion** to a 14-mark financial analysis question.

---

### 3.3 Sensitivity Analysis (Bonus — 2 Extra Marks Trick)

**What is it?** Sensitivity analysis tests "what if?" scenarios by varying one input (e.g., sales price, raw-material cost, discount rate) by ±10% to ±25% and re-computing NPV.

**Standard Sensitivity Table (for the RMC example):**

| Variable Changed | Change | New NPV at 12% | Project Still Viable? |
|------------------|-------:|---------------:|-----------------------|
| Sales price ↓ 10% | $-10\%$ | $+$₹ 9,40,000 | Yes |
| Raw material cost ↑ 10% | $+10\%$ | $+$₹ 16,20,000 | Yes |
| Discount rate ↑ to 18% | $+6\%$ | $+$₹ 4,44,000 | Yes |
| Cash inflow ↓ 20% | $-20\%$ | $-$₹ 2,80,000 | **No — Sensitive!** |

**Insight for KTU:** Mention the variable to which NPV is **most sensitive** — this is called the **"critical variable"** and is a high-value board point.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *Define Project Formulation. List any four elements of project formulation.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Project formulation is the systematic process of converting a business idea into a detailed, bankable investment proposal by analysing the market, technical, financial, managerial, and socio-environmental dimensions. It results in a Detailed Project Report (DPR).

**Four Elements (½ mark each):**
1. Market Analysis
2. Technical Analysis
3. Financial Analysis
4. Managerial / Organisational Analysis

*(Acceptable alternatives: Socio-Environmental Analysis, DPR Compilation, Project Appraisal)*

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Distinguish between Project Identification and Project Formulation.*

**Model Answer (Tabular form for 3 marks):**

| Basis | Project Identification | Project Formulation |
|------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Stage in Project Cycle** | First stage | Second stage |
| **Objective** | To generate and screen ideas | To convert a screened idea into a detailed proposal |
| **Key Question** | *What can we do?* | *How do we do it, and is it viable?* |
| **Output** | List of feasible ideas | Detailed Project Report (DPR) |
| **Tools Used** | Brainstorming, SWOT, gap analysis | Feasibility study, NPV/IRR, PERT/CPM |
| **Time & Cost** | Low | High |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**
> *(a)* Explain in detail the **six elements of project formulation** with their significance. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* A small-scale entrepreneur plans to invest **₹ 50,00,000** in a project that generates an annual after-tax cash inflow of **₹ 12,00,000** for **5 years**. The salvage value at the end of year 5 is **₹ 5,00,000** and the cost of capital is **10%**. Evaluate the project using **NPV, IRR, and BCR** with detailed calculations. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Six Elements of Project Formulation (7 marks)**

**1. Market Analysis (1.5 marks):**
Market analysis establishes the demand-supply gap, estimates the market size, identifies target customers, and forecasts sales. Techniques include *demand forecasting* (time-series, regression, end-use), *competitor analysis*, and *marketing mix (4Ps)*. Significance: prevents over- or under-capacity decisions.

**2. Technical Analysis (1.5 marks):**
Covers *plant location* (factor-rating, break-even location), *plant capacity* (installed vs. utilised), *technology selection* (manual/semi-automatic/fully automatic), *raw material sourcing*, and *infrastructure requirements*. Significance: ensures the project is technically feasible and scalable.

**3. Financial Analysis (1.5 marks):**
Quantifies the investment required, sources of finance, profitability projections, and applies *investment evaluation criteria* — Payback Period, NPV, IRR, BCR, DSCR. Significance: determines the financial viability and bankability of the project.

**4. Managerial / Organisational Analysis (1 mark):**
Specifies the *ownership structure* (proprietorship, partnership, LLP, Pvt. Ltd.), *organisation chart*, *manpower plan* (skill, number, cost), and *key managerial personnel*. Significance: ensures the venture has the human capital to execute the plan.

**5. Socio-Environmental Analysis (1 mark):**
Covers *Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)*, *statutory clearances* (Kerala State Pollution Control Board, FSSAI, Factory Inspectorate), *employment generation*, and *CSR obligations* under Section 135 of the Companies Act 2013. Significance: ensures legal compliance and social acceptance.

**6. Detailed Project Report — DPR (½ mark):**
The integrated document combining all five analyses, formatted as a bankable proposal typically running 80–150 pages. Significance: it is the final deliverable required by banks, VCs, and government agencies for sanction.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- $I_0 = $ ₹ 50,00,000
- $CF_t = $ ₹ 12,00,000 per year (years 1–5)
- Salvage value $S = $ ₹ 5,00,000 at year 5
- Discount rate $r = $ 10%
- $n = $ 5 years

---

**Step 1 — Compute NPV (2.5 marks):**

**1.1 — Annuity factor at 10% for 5 years:**

$$
PVIFA(10\%, 5) = \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-5}}{0.10} = \frac{1 - 0.6209}{0.10} = 3.7908
$$

**1.2 — PV of salvage value (year 5):**

$$
PV(S) = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{(1.10)^5} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1.6105} = 3{,}10{,}462
$$

**1.3 — Total PV of inflows:**

$$
PV(\text{Inflows}) = (12{,}00{,}000 \times 3.7908) + 3{,}10{,}462 = 45{,}48{,}960 + 3{,}10{,}462 = 48{,}59{,}422
$$

**1.4 — NPV:**

$$
NPV = 48{,}59{,}422 - 50{,}00{,}000 = -1{,}40{,}578
$$

**Step 2 — Compute IRR by trial (2.5 marks):**

**Try r = 8%:**

$$
PVIFA(8\%, 5) = \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-5}}{0.08} = \frac{1 - 0.6806}{0.08} = 3.9927
$$

$$
PV(\text{Salvage at 8\%}) = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{1.4693} = 3{,}40{,}291
$$

$$
PV(\text{Inflows at 8\%}) = (12{,}00{,}000 \times 3.9927) + 3{,}40{,}291 = 47{,}91{,}240 + 3{,}40{,}291 = 51{,}31{,}531
$$

$$
NPV_{8\%} = 51{,}31{,}531 - 50{,}00{,}000 = +1{,}31{,}531 \text{ (positive)}
$$

**Try r = 10% (already computed):** NPV = **−1,40,578** (negative)

**Interpolation:**

$$
IRR = 8\% + \frac{1{,}31{,}531}{1{,}31{,}531 - (-1{,}40{,}578)} \times (10\% - 8\%)
$$

$$
IRR = 8\% + \frac{1{,}31{,}531}{2{,}72{,}109} \times 2\% = 8\% + 0.966\% = 8.97\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute BCR (1.5 marks):**

$$
BCR = \frac{PV(\text{Inflows at 10\%})}{I_0} = \frac{48{,}59{,}422}{50{,}00{,}000} = 0.972
$$

**Step 4 — Combined Decision (½ mark):**

| Criterion | Value | Decision Rule | Result |
|-----------|------:|---------------|--------|
| NPV | −₹ 1,40,578 | $NPV \geq 0$ | **Reject** |
| IRR | 8.97% | $IRR \geq 10\%$ | **Reject** |
| BCR | 0.972 | $BCR \geq 1$ | **Reject** |

**Conclusion:** The project should be **rejected** at the 10% cost of capital, as all three criteria unanimously indicate non-viability. The entrepreneur should either reduce initial investment, increase cash inflows, or secure cheaper capital (cost $\leq 8.97\%$).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *(a)* What is a **Detailed Project Report (DPR)**? Explain its **contents and significance** in the context of bank financing. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Discuss the **role of financial analysis** in project formulation. With a suitable example, explain how **NPV and IRR** are used to take a project investment decision. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Detailed Project Report (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Detailed Project Report (DPR) is a comprehensive document that consolidates the outcomes of all feasibility studies — market, technical, financial, managerial, and environmental — into a single, structured, bankable investment proposal.

**Contents (4 marks — list with one-line explanation each):**

1. **Executive Summary** — A 1-page snapshot of the project idea, market, financials, and risk.
2. **Project Profile & Promoter Details** — Background, qualifications, and track record of the entrepreneur.
3. **Industry & Market Analysis** — Demand-supply gap, target market, competition, marketing strategy.
4. **Technical Plan** — Production process, technology, plant capacity, raw materials, utilities.
5. **Location & Infrastructure** — Site selection rationale, transport, water, power, communication.
6. **Project Cost & Means of Finance** — Capital cost, working capital, debt-equity ratio.
7. **Financial Projections** — P&L, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow for 5–10 years, sensitivity analysis.
8. **Profitability & Viability Indicators** — NPV, IRR, BCR, PBP, DSCR, breakeven point.
9. **Organisation & HR Plan** — Ownership pattern, management structure, manpower requirement.
10. **Implementation Schedule** — Bar/Gantt chart, milestones, critical path (CPM/PERT).
11. **Statutory & Environmental Compliances** — Licences, EIA, pollution control, labour laws.
12. **Risk Analysis & Mitigation** — Identification of risks and contingency plans.
13. **Annexures** — Quotations, certificates, MoUs, technical drawings.

**Significance in Bank Financing (2 marks):**

- **Mandatory document** for term-loan sanction by all scheduled commercial banks in India (RBI Master Circular on lending to MSME).
- Enables the banker to assess the **5 Cs of Credit** — Character, Capacity, Capital, Collateral, Conditions.
- Forms the basis for **DSCR (Debt-Service Coverage Ratio)** computation, the key bank-side decision metric.
- Required for **subsidy processing** under PMEGP, MUDRA, Stand-Up India, and state-level schemes (KSFE, KSIDC).
- Provides a **legal and financial roadmap** that protects both the entrepreneur and the lender.

---

#### **Part (b) — Role of Financial Analysis with NPV & IRR (7 marks)**

**Role of Financial Analysis (3 marks):**
Financial analysis is the *quantitative backbone* of project formulation. It translates qualitative insights from market and technical studies into monetary terms, enabling objective investment decisions. Its key roles are:
1. **Determines capital requirement** and optimal debt-equity mix.
2. **Assesses profitability** through P&L and cash flow projections.
3. **Applies evaluation criteria** (NPV, IRR, BCR, PBP, DSCR) to accept/reject projects.
4. **Tests risk** through sensitivity and scenario analysis.
5. **Enables comparison** between mutually exclusive investment alternatives.

**Illustrative Example (4 marks):**

Consider two competing projects **A** and **B**, each requiring an initial investment of **₹ 10,00,000**, with a discount rate of **10%** and a life of **4 years**.

| Year | Project A Cash Flow | Project B Cash Flow |
|-----:|--------------------:|--------------------:|
| 1 | ₹ 3,00,000 | ₹ 1,50,000 |
| 2 | ₹ 3,50,000 | ₹ 3,00,000 |
| 3 | ₹ 4,00,000 | ₹ 4,50,000 |
| 4 | ₹ 3,50,000 | ₹ 6,00,000 |

**NPV of Project A (at 10%):**

$$
NPV_A = -10{,}00{,}000 + \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.10} + \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{1.10^2} + \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{1.10^3} + \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{1.10^4}
$$

**Computation step-by-step:**

$$
NPV_A = -10{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}72{,}727 + 2{,}89{,}256 + 3{,}00{,}526 + 2{,}39{,}121
$$

$$
NPV_A = -10{,}00{,}000 + 11{,}01{,}630 = +\text{₹ }1{,}01{,}630
$$

**NPV of Project B (at 10%):**

$$
NPV_B = -10{,}00{,}000 + \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{1.10} + \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.10^2} + \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{1.10^3} + \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{1.10^4}
$$

**Computation step-by-step:**

$$
NPV_B = -10{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}36{,}364 + 2{,}47{,}934 + 3{,}38{,}092 + 4{,}09{,}908
$$

$$
NPV_B = -10{,}00{,}000 + 11{,}32{,}298 = +\text{₹ }1{,}32{,}298
$$

**Decision:** Since $NPV_B > NPV_A$, **Project B is preferred** when both are independent and not mutually exclusive. If they were mutually exclusive, Project B would still be selected.

**IRR Comparison (½ mark insight):**
A project with **higher NPV at the same cost of capital** is generally preferred. IRR should be used with caution when cash flows change signs (unconventional cash flows), as it can give multiple solutions. **NPV remains the theoretically superior and KTU-preferred criterion.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing NPV with Profit:** NPV is *present value* based, not accounting profit. Do not use P&L figures; use only cash flows.
> 2. **Forgetting Salvage Value:** In payback/NPV, salvage must be added to the final year's cash flow before discounting.
> 3. **IRR Interpolation Error:** Always ensure the lower rate gives positive NPV and the higher rate gives negative NPV before interpolating. A reverse sign leads to wrong IRR.
> 4. **Mixing up DPR contents:** Students often list "DPR" and "Project Formulation" as the same. DPR is the *output*; formulation is the *process*.
> 5. **Ignoring Working Capital:** A common omission. Working capital is part of the initial investment in NPV/PBP calculations.
> 6. **Writing Theory Without Structure:** For 7-mark sub-parts, use **bold headings, bullet points, and formulas**. Pure paragraphs lose 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Decimal and Unit Mistakes:** Always carry two decimal places in discount factors. ₹ symbol is optional but units (₹, %, years) are mandatory.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as a 60-second last-minute revision before the KTU exam.**

**✅ Core Concepts**
- Project Formulation = converting an idea into a bankable DPR.
- Six pillars: Market, Technical, Financial, Managerial, Socio-Environmental, DPR.
- DPR is the *output*; Formulation is the *process*.
- Identification → Formulation → Appraisal → Implementation.

**✅ Formula Quick-Fire List**
- $PBP = I_0 / CF_t$
- $NPV = \sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ — Accept if $NPV \geq 0$
- $IRR$ solves $\sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ — Accept if $IRR \geq r$
- $BCR = \sum \frac{B_t}{(1+r)^t} / \sum \frac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ — Accept if $BCR \geq 1$
- $DSCR \geq 1.5$ is the bank-norm.
- $PVIFA(r,n) = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$

**✅ Numerical Answer Strategy (7-mark sub-part)**
1. Write given data in a small table.
2. Compute annuity factor with explicit substitution.
3. Discount salvage value separately.
4. Show NPV calculation in two steps (PV inflows → minus I₀).
5. Show IRR via trial-and-error (try 8% and 12% or similar) + interpolation formula.
6. End with a clear "Accept / Reject" decision and a 2-line justification.

**✅ Theory Answer Strategy (7-mark sub-part)**
1. Bold-heading each element.
2. One-line definition + one-line significance.
3. Cite at least one example (Kerala-specific if possible — KSIDC, KSFE, PMEGP).
4. Close with a 1-line summary.

**✅ Common Examiner Triggers**
- "Explain the elements of project formulation" → List all six with significance.
- "What is a DPR?" → Definition + Contents (13 items) + Significance.
- Numerical: "Evaluate using NPV/IRR/BCR" → All three + final combined decision.
- "Distinguish between Identification and Formulation" → Tabular form preferred.

**✅ Kerala-Specific Industry Hooks (Use in Answers for Extra Marks)**
- **Kerala State Industrial Development Corporation (KSIDC)** — provides DPR-based term loans.
- **Kerala Financial Corporation (KFC)** — funds MSMEs with DPR-mandated appraisal.
- **Kerala Startup Mission (KSUM)** — supports student entrepreneurs with seed funding based on DPRs.
- **PMEGP** — DPR is mandatory for subsidy eligibility.

**✅ Frequently Asked Definitions (Memorise Verbatim)**
- *Project:* A planned series of activities with a definite start and end, undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result.
- *Project Formulation:* The systematic preparation of a detailed investment proposal.
- *DPR:* A comprehensive, integrated document containing all feasibility outcomes.
- *Feasibility Study:* An evaluation of a project's viability across multiple dimensions.
- *Appraisal:* An independent review of the formulated project by a financing agency.

**✅ Common Pitfalls to Avoid (See Warning Callout Above)**
- NPV uses cash flows, not accounting profits.
- Include salvage value in final-year cash flow.
- For mutually exclusive projects, prefer higher NPV, not higher IRR.
- Always compute DSCR for bank-financed projects.
- Sensitivity analysis is not optional in professional DPRs.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Elements of Project Formulation | KTU 2024 Scheme | OEEVT614 Entrepreneurship**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Design and Network Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT614 - ENTREPRENEURSHIP
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Project Design and Network Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧭 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Project Design?

**Project Design** is the structured process of translating an entrepreneurial idea or business opportunity into a clearly defined, executable blueprint. It involves outlining objectives, deliverables, timelines, resource requirements, technical specifications, and risk management strategies before a single rupee is invested.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (OEEVT614):**
> *Project Design is the systematic planning of an enterprise venture that integrates feasibility analysis, technical architecture, work breakdown structure, schedule, and budget into a single coherent document used to attract investors and guide execution.*

### 1.2 What is Network Analysis?

**Network Analysis** is a quantitative project management technique that models a project as a network of interconnected activities and events. It is used to determine the **minimum project completion time**, identify the **critical path**, and compute **activity slacks (floats)** so that the entrepreneur can plan, monitor, and control time-bound execution.

The two principal branches of Network Analysis used in KTU syllabus are:

| Technique | Full Form | Key Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **CPM** | Critical Path Method | Deterministic — single time estimate per activity |
| **PERT** | Program Evaluation & Review Technique | Probabilistic — three time estimates (Optimistic, Most Likely, Pessimistic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme requires students to draw a network diagram (AOA — Activity on Arrow), compute Earliest & Latest event times, identify the **Critical Path**, and calculate all three floats (Total, Free, Independent).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Train Journey" Analogy 🚂

Imagine a railway network with multiple stations (events) and train routes (activities) between them.

- Each **station** = an **Event** (a milestone where one or more activities start/finish).
- Each **train route** = an **Activity** (a piece of work that consumes time).
- Some routes share stations — this is **network dependency**.
- The **longest route** from the originating station to the destination = **Critical Path** (the train that determines when the journey as a whole ends).
- If a parallel train has a shorter route, it can **wait (float/slack)** without delaying the whole journey.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for an Entrepreneur:** Network analysis prevents the "fire-fighting" syndrome — the chaos that arises when an entrepreneur has no idea which task is the bottleneck. It transforms intuition-driven scheduling into a data-driven, investor-defensible plan.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Activity** – Any identifiable task consuming time and resources.
- **Event (Node)** – A point in time marking the start or finish of one or more activities. Represented as a **circle** or **numbered node**.
- **Dummy Activity** – A fictitious activity with **zero duration** used purely to maintain logical dependencies in the network.
- **Critical Path** – The **longest path** through the network; it determines the **minimum project duration**.
- **Float / Slack** – The amount of time a non-critical activity can be delayed without delaying the project.
- **Earliest Event Time (EET / TE)** – The earliest time at which an event can occur.
- **Latest Event Time (LET / TL)** – The latest time at which an event can occur without delaying the project.
- **Standard Project Duration Unit:** **days** (unless otherwise specified).
- **Standard Cost Metric:** **Rupees (₹)** per day for crashing decisions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Steps of Network Analysis (KTU Board Procedure)

1. **Define the Project Scope** and list all activities.
2. **Establish Precedence Relationships** — which activity must precede which.
3. **Estimate Activity Durations** (single value for CPM; three values for PERT).
4. **Construct the Network Diagram** using **AOA (Activity on Arrow)** notation.
5. **Number the Events** using Fulkerson's Rule (every arrow's tail number < head number).
6. **Compute Earliest Event Times (EET)** — Forward Pass.
7. **Compute Latest Event Times (LET)** — Backward Pass.
8. **Calculate Floats** for each activity.
9. **Identify the Critical Path** — activities with **Total Float = 0**.
10. **Determine Project Duration** = EET of the final event.

## 2.2 Rules for Drawing an AOA Network

- An event is represented as a **circle** with a unique number.
- An activity is represented as a **directed arrow** between two events.
- **No two activities can have the same start and end events** — use a dummy activity.
- **Loops (cycles) are not permitted** in a project network.
- The network should have a **single start event** and a **single end event**.
- Event numbers must increase along the direction of arrows.

## 2.3 Dummy Activities — When and Why?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **dummy activity** has zero duration and zero cost. It exists solely to:
> 1. Show **logical dependencies** between activities (e.g., C needs A, D needs A, but E needs C only — a dummy avoids incorrect parallelism).
> 2. **Avoid duplicate event numbering** when two activities share the same start and end.

## 2.4 PERT — Three Time Estimates

For each activity, three estimates are collected:
- $a$ = Optimistic time
- $m$ = Most Likely time
- $b$ = Pessimistic time

These are combined into a single **Expected Time** and a **Variance** for statistical probability calculations.

## 2.5 ⭐ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table. These are the **only** formulas KTU examiners test in this module.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Expected Time (PERT) | $t_e = \dfrac{a + 4m + b}{6}$ | Beta distribution approximation |
| 2 | Variance (PERT) | $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{b - a}{6}\right)^2$ | Measures time uncertainty |
| 3 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \dfrac{b - a}{6}$ | Square root of variance |
| 4 | Earliest Event Time (EET) | $EET(j) = \max_i \{ EET(i) + t_{ij} \}$ | Forward pass — at node $j$ |
| 5 | Latest Event Time (LET) | $LET(i) = \min_j \{ LET(j) - t_{ij} \}$ | Backward pass — at node $i$ |
| 6 | Total Float (TF) | $TF = LET(j) - EET(i) - t_{ij}$ | Delay without delaying project |
| 7 | Free Float (FF) | $FF = EET(j) - EET(i) - t_{ij}$ | Delay without delaying successor |
| 8 | Independent Float (IF) | $IF = \max(0,\; EET(j) - LET(i) - t_{ij})$ | Delay without affecting any float |
| 9 | Project Variance (PERT) | $\sigma_p^2 = \sum \sigma^2_{\text{critical path}}$ | Sum of variances on critical path |
| 10 | Z-value (Probability) | $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_p}$ | $T_s$ = scheduled, $T_e$ = expected |
| 11 | Crash Cost Slope | $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{C_c - C_n}{T_n - T_c}$ | $C$ = cost, $T$ = time, $c$=crash, $n$=normal |
| 12 | Probability | $P(Z \leq z) = \Phi(z)$ | From Standard Normal Table |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "TOM FLY" for Floats:** **T**otal Float uses **L**atest (LET). **F**ree Float uses **E**arliest (EET). **I**ndependent uses **E**arliest end and **L**atest start.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Entrepreneurship

- **Construction startups** use CPM to plan multi-storey projects.
- **Hardware product startups** use PERT to manage R&D uncertainty.
- **Software-tech ventures** use Gantt charts (a derivative of network analysis) for sprint planning.
- **Manufacturing MSMEs** use crashing analysis to evaluate make-vs-buy decisions under deadline pressure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Comprehensive Network Analysis

### 📋 Problem Statement

A small entrepreneurship project consists of **8 activities (A–H)** with the following logical relationships and durations (in days):

| Activity | Predecessor(s) | Duration |
|---|---|---|
| A | None | 2 |
| B | None | 3 |
| C | A | 3 |
| D | A | 3 |
| E | B | 3 |
| F | C, D | 4 |
| G | E | 2 |
| H | F, G | 3 |

> **Required:** Draw the network, find the **Critical Path**, **Project Duration**, and compute **Total Float, Free Float, Independent Float** for all activities.

---

### 🔷 Step 1: Construct the AOA Network

From the precedence list, we observe:
- **A and B** start the project.
- **C and D** both follow A → they share start event but have different end events.
- **F** needs **both C and D** completed.
- **G** follows E.
- **H** needs **both F and G** completed.

To correctly merge C & D before F, and F & G before H, we introduce **two dummy activities (d1 and d2)**.

**Node Map:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&1 \xrightarrow{A} 2 \quad (t_A = 2) \\
&1 \xrightarrow{B} 3 \quad (t_B = 3) \\
&2 \xrightarrow{C} 4 \quad (t_C = 3) \\
&2 \xrightarrow{D} 5 \quad (t_D = 3) \\
&3 \xrightarrow{E} 6 \quad (t_E = 3) \\
&4 \xrightarrow{d_1} 5 \quad (t_{d_1} = 0) \\
&5 \xrightarrow{F} 7 \quad (t_F = 4) \\
&6 \xrightarrow{G} 8 \quad (t_G = 2) \\
&7 \xrightarrow{d_2} 8 \quad (t_{d_2} = 0) \\
&8 \xrightarrow{H} 9 \quad (t_H = 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verification of Fulkerson's Rule:** For every arrow, tail number < head number. ✅

---

### 🔷 Step 2: Forward Pass — Compute Earliest Event Time (EET)

**Rule:** $EET(j) = \max_i \{ EET(i) + t_{ij} \}$ — computed left-to-right.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EET(1) &= 0 &&\text{(Project Start)} \\
EET(2) &= EET(1) + t_A = 0 + 2 = 2 \\
EET(3) &= EET(1) + t_B = 0 + 3 = 3 \\
EET(4) &= EET(2) + t_C = 2 + 3 = 5 \\
EET(5) &= \max\{EET(2) + t_D,\; EET(4) + t_{d_1}\} = \max\{2+3,\; 5+0\} = \max\{5,5\} = 5 \\
EET(6) &= EET(3) + t_E = 3 + 3 = 6 \\
EET(7) &= EET(5) + t_F = 5 + 4 = 9 \\
EET(8) &= \max\{EET(6) + t_G,\; EET(7) + t_{d_2}\} = \max\{6+2,\; 9+0\} = \max\{8,9\} = 9 \\
EET(9) &= EET(8) + t_H = 9 + 3 = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **Project Duration = EET(9) = 12 days**

---

### 🔷 Step 3: Backward Pass — Compute Latest Event Time (LET)

**Rule:** $LET(i) = \min_j \{ LET(j) - t_{ij} \}$ — computed right-to-left.

$$
\begin{aligned}
LET(9) &= EET(9) = 12 &&\text{(Project End — set equal to EET)} \\
LET(8) &= LET(9) - t_H = 12 - 3 = 9 \\
LET(7) &= LET(8) - t_{d_2} = 9 - 0 = 9 \\
LET(6) &= LET(8) - t_G = 9 - 2 = 7 \\
LET(5) &= LET(7) - t_F = 9 - 4 = 5 \\
LET(4) &= LET(5) - t_{d_1} = 5 - 0 = 5 \\
LET(3) &= LET(6) - t_E = 7 - 3 = 4 \\
LET(2) &= \min\{LET(4) - t_C,\; LET(5) - t_D\} = \min\{5-3,\; 5-3\} = \min\{2,2\} = 2 \\
LET(1) &= \min\{LET(2) - t_A,\; LET(3) - t_B\} = \min\{2-2,\; 4-3\} = \min\{0,1\} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **LET(1) = 0** confirms the schedule is feasible (no negative values).

---

### 🔷 Step 4: Compute the Three Floats

For each activity $(i, j)$ with duration $t_{ij}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TF_{ij} &= LET(j) - EET(i) - t_{ij} \\
FF_{ij} &= EET(j) - EET(i) - t_{ij} \\
IF_{ij} &= \max\bigl(0,\; EET(j) - LET(i) - t_{ij}\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 📊 Master Float Table (KTU Board Format)

| Activity | $i \to j$ | $t_{ij}$ | $EET(i)$ | $EET(j)$ | $LET(i)$ | $LET(j)$ | TF | FF | IF | Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 1→2 | 2 | 0 | 2 | 0 | 2 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| B | 1→3 | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 | No |
| C | 2→4 | 3 | 2 | 5 | 2 | 5 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| D | 2→5 | 3 | 2 | 5 | 2 | 5 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| E | 3→6 | 3 | 3 | 6 | 4 | 7 | 1 | 0 | 0 | No |
| F | 5→7 | 4 | 5 | 9 | 5 | 9 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| G | 6→8 | 2 | 6 | 9 | 7 | 9 | 1 | 1 | 0 | No |
| H | 8→9 | 3 | 9 | 12 | 9 | 12 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| d1 | 4→5 | 0 | 5 | 5 | 5 | 5 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✅ Yes |
| d2 | 7→8 | 0 | 9 | 9 | 9 | 9 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✅ Yes |

> **Critical Path:** Activities with **Total Float = 0** form the critical path.
> 
> Path traced through events: **1 → 2 → 5 → 7 → 8 → 9** (or equivalently 1→2→4→5→7→8→9)
> 
> **Critical Path Activities:** A — D — F — H (with d1, d2 as necessary bridges)
> 
> **Critical Path Duration:** $2 + 3 + 4 + 3 = 12$ days ✅

---

### 🔷 Step 5: PERT Extension — Three Time Estimates

Suppose activity estimates are:

| Activity | $a$ | $m$ | $b$ | $t_e = (a+4m+b)/6$ | $\sigma^2 = ((b-a)/6)^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 1 | 2 | 3 | 2.00 | 0.111 |
| B | 2 | 3 | 4 | 3.00 | 0.111 |
| C | 1 | 2 | 9 | 3.00 | 1.778 |
| D | 2 | 2 | 8 | 3.00 | 1.000 |
| E | 2 | 3 | 4 | 3.00 | 0.111 |
| F | 3 | 4 | 5 | 4.00 | 0.111 |
| G | 1 | 2 | 3 | 2.00 | 0.111 |
| H | 1 | 2 | 9 | 3.00 | 1.778 |

**Project Variance (on critical path A–D–F–H):**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = 0.111 + 1.000 + 0.111 + 1.778 = 3.000
$$

$$
\sigma_p = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732
$$

**Probability of completing in 14 days:**

$$
Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_p} = \frac{14 - 12}{1.732} = 1.155
$$

From Standard Normal Table: $P(Z \leq 1.155) \approx 0.876$, i.e., **87.6% probability** of finishing in 14 days.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** KTU evaluators love step-by-step substitution in PERT problems. Always show the formula, then the substitution, then the numerical result — never skip a step.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Network Analysis Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Step 1: Define Project Scope] --> stepB[Step 2: List All Activities]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Identify Predecessors]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Estimate Durations]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Draw AOA Network]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Forward Pass - EET]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7: Backward Pass - LET]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8: Calculate Floats]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 9: Identify Critical Path]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 10: Determine Project Duration]
    stepJ --> stepK{Project on Schedule?}
    stepK -->|Yes| stepL[Monitor and Execute]
    stepK -->|No| stepM[Apply Project Crashing]
    stepM --> stepJ
```

## 4.2 AOA Network Diagram for the Worked Example

```mermaid
graph LR
    n1((1)) -->|A = 2| n2((2))
    n1((1)) -->|B = 3| n3((3))
    n2((2)) -->|C = 3| n4((4))
    n2((2)) -->|D = 3| n5((5))
    n3((3)) -->|E = 3| n6((6))
    n4((4)) -->|d1 = 0| n5((5))
    n5((5)) -->|F = 4| n7((7))
    n6((6)) -->|G = 2| n8((8))
    n7((7)) -->|d2 = 0| n8((8))
    n8((8)) -->|H = 3| n9((9))
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each node (circle) is an event. Each arrow is an activity with its duration labeled. The arrows $d_1$ and $d_2$ are **dummy activities** (zero duration) used to maintain logical dependencies.

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: CPM vs PERT

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Project Idea] --> branchA{Certainty of Duration?}
    branchA -->|Deterministic| cpmPath[CPM Branch]
    branchA -->|Probabilistic| pertPath[PERT Branch]
    
    cpmPath --> cpm1[Single Time Estimate per Activity]
    cpm1 --> cpm2[Focus on Cost-Time Tradeoff]
    cpm2 --> cpm3[Identify Critical Path]
    cpm3 --> cpm4[Apply Crashing for Optimization]
    
    pertPath --> pert1[Three Estimates: Optimistic Most Likely Pessimistic]
    pert1 --> pert2[Compute Expected Time and Variance]
    pert2 --> pert3[Identify Critical Path]
    pert3 --> pert4[Compute Probability of Completion]
    
    cpm4 --> final[Optimal Project Plan]
    pert4 --> final
```

## 4.4 Critical Path Visualisation

```mermaid
graph LR
    cp1((1)) ==>|A CRITICAL| cp2((2))
    cp2((2)) ==>|D CRITICAL| cp3((5))
    cp3((5)) ==>|F CRITICAL| cp4((7))
    cp4((7)) -.->|d2| cp5((8))
    cp5((8)) ==>|H CRITICAL| cp6((9))
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **bold/double-line arrows** indicate the **Critical Path** — the chain of activities where any delay directly delays the entire project. Entrepreneurs must monitor these activities with zero slack **hour-by-hour**.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define a **Dummy Activity**. State any two situations where it is used in network construction.  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Dummy Activity** is a fictitious activity in a project network that has **zero duration** and **zero cost**. It is represented as a dashed arrow in the AOA diagram and consumes no resources.

**Two situations where it is used:**

1. **To maintain logical dependencies:** When two activities share a common predecessor and one of them is also a predecessor of another activity, a dummy is required to show the correct dependency chain. *Example:* In our worked example, dummy $d_1$ shows that F depends on D's completion via node 5.

2. **To avoid duplicate activity identification:** When two activities have identical start and end events, a dummy ensures unique event numbering (per Fulkerson's Rule). It prevents confusion during forward and backward pass calculations.

---

### **Q2.** Distinguish between **CPM** and **PERT**.  `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | CPM (Critical Path Method) | PERT (Program Evaluation & Review Technique) |
|---|---|---|
| Time Estimates | Single deterministic value | Three estimates (Optimistic, Most Likely, Pessimistic) |
| Orientation | **Cost–Time** trade-off focused | **Probability / Risk** focused |
| Suitable for | Repetitive, well-defined projects (construction, manufacturing) | R&D, novel, uncertain projects (product development, research) |
| Model Used | Deterministic | Probabilistic (Beta distribution) |
| Crashing Analysis | Yes — primary purpose | Less common; mainly uses expected time |
| Output | Critical Path, Project Duration, Crashing plan | Critical Path, Expected Duration, Probability of on-time completion |
| Developed by | DuPont & Remington Rand (1957) | U.S. Navy Polaris Project (1958) |

---

## 📌 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** Students must answer **one full question** of 14 marks with **internal choice** between Q1 and Q2 (or Q3 and Q4, depending on slot).

---

### 🔹 **Question A (14 Marks)** — Full Network Analysis Problem

A small-scale food processing startup is launching a new product. The project has been broken into **7 activities** with the following data:

| Activity | Predecessor(s) | Duration (days) |
|---|---|---|
| A | — | 4 |
| B | — | 6 |
| C | A | 5 |
| D | A | 3 |
| E | B | 4 |
| F | C, D | 6 |
| G | E, F | 5 |

#### **(a) Construct the AOA network diagram. Number the events and identify all activities including any dummies required.  [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

From the predecessor list:
- A and B start the project.
- C and D both follow A (parallel activities from A).
- F requires both C and D to finish.
- G requires both E and F to finish.

**Network construction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&1 \xrightarrow{A=4} 2 \\
&1 \xrightarrow{B=6} 3 \\
&2 \xrightarrow{C=5} 4 \\
&2 \xrightarrow{D=3} 5 \\
&3 \xrightarrow{E=4} 6 \\
&4 \xrightarrow{d_1=0} 5 \quad \text{(dummy to merge C and D before F)} \\
&5 \xrightarrow{F=6} 7 \\
&6 \xrightarrow{d_2=0} 7 \quad \text{(dummy to merge E and F before G)} \\
&7 \xrightarrow{G=5} 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** All arrow tail numbers < head numbers (Fulkerson's rule). ✅

**Total events:** 8 (numbered 1 to 8)
**Total activities (including dummies):** 9

---

#### **(b) Perform forward and backward pass. Identify the critical path and project duration. Calculate Total Float, Free Float, and Independent Float for all activities.  [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Forward Pass (EET):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EET(1) &= 0 \\
EET(2) &= 0 + 4 = 4 \\
EET(3) &= 0 + 6 = 6 \\
EET(4) &= 4 + 5 = 9 \\
EET(5) &= \max(4+3,\; 9+0) = \max(7,9) = 9 \\
EET(6) &= 6 + 4 = 10 \\
EET(7) &= \max(9+6,\; 10+0) = \max(15,10) = 15 \\
EET(8) &= 15 + 5 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project Duration = 20 days**

**Backward Pass (LET):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LET(8) &= 20 \\
LET(7) &= 20 - 5 = 15 \\
LET(6) &= 15 - 0 = 15 \\
LET(5) &= 15 - 6 = 9 \\
LET(4) &= 9 - 0 = 9 \\
LET(3) &= 15 - 4 = 11 \\
LET(2) &= \min(9-5,\; 9-3) = \min(4,6) = 4 \\
LET(1) &= \min(4-4,\; 11-6) = \min(0,5) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Float Table:**

| Activity | $i \to j$ | $t$ | $EET(i)$ | $EET(j)$ | $LET(i)$ | $LET(j)$ | TF | FF | IF | Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 1→2 | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| B | 1→3 | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 11 | 5 | 0 | 0 | No |
| C | 2→4 | 5 | 4 | 9 | 4 | 9 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| D | 2→5 | 3 | 4 | 9 | 4 | 9 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| E | 3→6 | 4 | 6 | 10 | 11 | 15 | 5 | 0 | 0 | No |
| F | 5→7 | 6 | 9 | 15 | 9 | 15 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| G | 7→8 | 5 | 15 | 20 | 15 | 20 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| d1 | 4→5 | 0 | 9 | 9 | 9 | 9 | **0** | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| d2 | 6→7 | 0 | 10 | 15 | 15 | 15 | **0** | 5 | 0 | No |

**Critical Path:** 1 → 2 → 4 → 5 → 7 → 8 (via activities **A → C → d1 → F → G**)
**Critical Path Duration:** $4 + 5 + 0 + 6 + 5 = 20$ days ✅

---

### 🔹 **Question B (14 Marks)** — PERT Probability Analysis

A biotech startup is planning a new vaccine R&D project with **6 activities**. The three time estimates (in weeks) are:

| Activity | Predecessor | Optimistic $a$ | Most Likely $m$ | Pessimistic $b$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | — | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| B | — | 1 | 2 | 9 |
| C | A | 3 | 5 | 7 |
| D | A | 2 | 3 | 10 |
| E | B, C | 4 | 5 | 12 |
| F | D, E | 1 | 3 | 5 |

#### **(a) Calculate the Expected Time and Variance for each activity. Construct the network diagram.  [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $t_e$ and $\sigma^2$ for each activity:**

$$
t_e = \frac{a + 4m + b}{6}, \qquad \sigma^2 = \left(\frac{b-a}{6}\right)^2
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_e(A) &= \frac{2 + 16 + 6}{6} = 4.00, \quad \sigma^2_A = \left(\frac{6-2}{6}\right)^2 = 0.444 \\
t_e(B) &= \frac{1 + 8 + 9}{6} = 3.00, \quad \sigma^2_B = \left(\frac{9-1}{6}\right)^2 = 1.778 \\
t_e(C) &= \frac{3 + 20 + 7}{6} = 5.00, \quad \sigma^2_C = \left(\frac{7-3}{6}\right)^2 = 0.444 \\
t_e(D) &= \frac{2 + 12 + 10}{6} = 4.00, \quad \sigma^2_D = \left(\frac{10-2}{6}\right)^2 = 1.778 \\
t_e(E) &= \frac{4 + 20 + 12}{6} = 6.00, \quad \sigma^2_E = \left(\frac{12-4}{6}\right)^2 = 1.778 \\
t_e(F) &= \frac{1 + 12 + 5}{6} = 3.00, \quad \sigma^2_F = \left(\frac{5-1}{6}\right)^2 = 0.444
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Activity | $t_e$ (weeks) | $\sigma^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 4.0 | 0.444 |
| B | 3.0 | 1.778 |
| C | 5.0 | 0.444 |
| D | 4.0 | 1.778 |
| E | 6.0 | 1.778 |
| F | 3.0 | 0.444 |

**Step 2: Construct the network (using expected times):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&1 \xrightarrow{A=4} 2 \\
&1 \xrightarrow{B=3} 3 \\
&2 \xrightarrow{C=5} 4 \\
&2 \xrightarrow{D=4} 5 \\
&3 \xrightarrow{d_1=0} 4 \quad \text{(dummy to merge B and C before E)} \\
&4 \xrightarrow{E=6} 6 \\
&5 \xrightarrow{d_2=0} 6 \quad \text{(dummy to merge D and E before F)} \\
&6 \xrightarrow{F=3} 7
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **(b) Find the critical path and project duration. If the scheduled deadline is 17 weeks, find the probability of completing the project on time.  [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Forward Pass (EET):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EET(1) &= 0 \\
EET(2) &= 0 + 4 = 4 \\
EET(3) &= 0 + 3 = 3 \\
EET(4) &= \max(4+5,\; 3+0) = \max(9,3) = 9 \\
EET(5) &= 4 + 4 = 8 \\
EET(6) &= \max(9+6,\; 8+0) = \max(15,8) = 15 \\
EET(7) &= 15 + 3 = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project Duration $T_e = 18$ weeks**

**Backward Pass (LET):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LET(7) &= 18 \\
LET(6) &= 18 - 3 = 15 \\
LET(5) &= 15 - 0 = 15 \\
LET(4) &= 15 - 6 = 9 \\
LET(3) &= 9 - 0 = 9 \\
LET(2) &= \min(9-5,\; 15-4) = \min(4,11) = 4 \\
LET(1) &= \min(4-4,\; 9-3) = \min(0,6) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical Path Identification:**

| Activity | $t_e$ | TF |
|---|---|---|
| A | 4 | **0** ✅ |
| B | 3 | 6 |
| C | 5 | **0** ✅ |
| D | 4 | 7 |
| E | 6 | **0** ✅ |
| F | 3 | **0** ✅ |
| d1 | 0 | **0** ✅ |
| d2 | 0 | 7 |

**Critical Path:** 1 → 2 → 4 → 6 → 7 (Activities: **A — C — d1 — E — F**)
**Path Duration:** $4 + 5 + 0 + 6 + 3 = 18$ weeks ✅

**Project Variance (sum of variances on critical path):**

$$
\sigma_p^2 = \sigma^2_A + \sigma^2_C + \sigma^2_{d_1} + \sigma^2_E + \sigma^2_F
$$

$$
\sigma_p^2 = 0.444 + 0.444 + 0 + 1.778 + 0.444 = 3.110
$$

$$
\sigma_p = \sqrt{3.110} \approx 1.764
$$

**Probability Calculation (for $T_s = 17$ weeks):**

$$
Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_p} = \frac{17 - 18}{1.764} = -0.567
$$

$$
P(Z \leq -0.567) = 1 - P(Z \leq 0.567) = 1 - 0.714 = 0.286
$$

✅ **Probability of completing in 17 weeks ≈ 28.6%**

**Interpretation for the entrepreneur:** The project has a less than 30% chance of meeting a 17-week deadline. The founder should either extend the deadline to ~20 weeks (to gain ~84% confidence) or apply **project crashing** on the critical path activities.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting Dummy Activities** → 2-mark penalty. Examiners will mark a "duplicate activity" as a structural error.
> 2. **Incorrect Float Formula** → Mixing up EET and LET. **TF uses LET; FF uses EET.**
> 3. **Ignoring Fulkerson's Rule** → Causes the entire network to be re-numbered; can cost 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping units** in PERT $Z$-value → Always mention "weeks" or "days."
> 5. **Wrong PERT Variance Sum** → Variance is summed **only on the critical path**, not the whole network.
> 6. **No Z-Table Reference** → Examiners expect you to mention "From Standard Normal Tables."

---

## 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Project Design** = structured blueprint of an entrepreneurial venture; integrates feasibility, scope, and resources.
- **Network Analysis** = quantitative project scheduling using **CPM** (deterministic) or **PERT** (probabilistic).
- **AOA Notation** uses **arrows = activities** and **circles = events**.
- **Fulkerson's Rule:** every arrow's tail number < head number — mandatory.
- **Dummy Activity** = zero duration; used to fix logical dependencies and avoid duplicate activities.
- **Forward Pass → EET** (max rule); **Backward Pass → LET** (min rule).
- **Floats:** TF (LET-based), FF (EET-based), IF (max of 0, EET(end) − LET(start) − duration).
- **Critical Path** = activities with **TF = 0**; determines **minimum project duration**.
- **PERT Expected Time:** $t_e = (a + 4m + b)/6$; **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = ((b-a)/6)^2$.
- **Project Variance** = sum of variances on the critical path.
- **Z-value:** $Z = (T_s - T_e) / \sigma_p$ → use **Standard Normal Table** to find probability.
- **Crashing** reduces duration at extra cost; choose the activity with the **lowest crash cost slope** on the critical path.
- **Gantt Chart** is a visual derivative of network analysis used for sprint/agile planning.
- **For KTU exams:** always show every arithmetic step, label every node, and use proper units (days/weeks).
- **Mnemonic for floats:** "**F**ree float = **F**ree to use without harming successor" — uses only EET.
- **Mnemonic for PERT:** "**a**lways **m**eet **b**oundaries" → $a, 4m, b$.
- **Golden Rule:** Only one network → only one critical path (or multiple parallel critical paths of equal length).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
