> - **Course:** OEEVT614 - ENTREPRENEURSHIP
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concept of Project Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Evaluation** is the systematic and scientific process of assessing a proposed investment project to determine its **technical feasibility**, **financial viability**, **economic desirability**, **social acceptability**, and **managerial operability** before, during, and after its execution. It is a critical decision-making tool used by entrepreneurs, financial institutions, and government bodies to allocate scarce resources optimally and to ensure that the project creates maximum value for stakeholders.

According to the **UNIDO (United Nations Industrial Development Organization) Guidelines**, project evaluation is defined as *"the total process of determining whether a project should be undertaken, of comparing alternative projects, and of evaluating the consequences of implementing the selected project."*

The **three pillars** of project evaluation as per the **KTU 2024 Entrepreneurship Syllabus (Module 3)** are:
1. **Financial Analysis** (Quantitative, money-measured returns)
2. **Economic Analysis** (National welfare, shadow pricing)
3. **Social-Cost-Benefit Analysis** (Wider societal impact)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Buying a House**
> Imagine you have ₹50 Lakhs and are considering buying a house. Before signing the cheque, you would naturally:
> - Check if the **price is fair** (Financial Evaluation)
> - Check if the **location suits your work** (Technical/Operational Evaluation)
> - Check if the **neighborhood is safe and has good schools** (Social Evaluation)
> - Predict whether **resale value will rise** (Future Economic Evaluation)
> - Verify **legal documents** (Risk & Compliance Evaluation)
>
> **Project Evaluation is exactly this due-diligence process, but applied to a business venture or industrial project.**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Project Evaluation

The key yardsticks (covered in detail in Section 2) are:
- **Net Present Value (NPV)** — measured in monetary units (₹, $, etc.)
- **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** — measured in **%**
- **Payback Period** — measured in **years/months**
- **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** — dimensionless ratio
- **Profitability Index (PI)** — dimensionless ratio
- **Discount Rate / Cost of Capital (r)** — measured in **%**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must clearly differentiate between **Project Appraisal**, **Project Feasibility Study**, and **Project Evaluation** — these three terms are often confused in the exam and carry separate marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Project Evaluation (Time-Based)

Project evaluation is classified into **three temporal categories**, each with a distinct purpose:

### A. Ex-Ante Evaluation (Pre-Investment / Before the Project)
- Also called **Project Appraisal** or **Feasibility Study**.
- Conducted **before** committing capital.
- Answers: *"Should we do this project?"*
- Components: Market Study, Technical Study, Financial Study, Managerial Study, Environmental Study, Social-Cost Benefit Study.

### B. Interim / Concurrent Evaluation (During Implementation)
- Also called **Ongoing Evaluation** or **Mid-Term Appraisal**.
- Conducted **during** the project execution phase.
- Answers: *"Are we on track? Should we course-correct?"*
- Compares *actual performance* vs *planned performance*.

### C. Ex-Post Evaluation (After Completion)
- Also called **Terminal Evaluation** or **Post-Completion Audit**.
- Conducted **after** the project is operational.
- Answers: *"Did the project deliver what was promised?"*
- Generates lessons learned for future projects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the Exam: "E-I-E"** → **E**x-Ante → **I**nterim → **E**x-Post.

## 2.2 Components (Dimensions) of Project Evaluation

A complete project evaluation examines the project from **seven dimensions**:

| # | Dimension | Question Answered | Tools Used |
|---|-----------|-------------------|------------|
| 1 | **Market Analysis** | Is there a demand? | Market surveys, SWOT |
| 2 | **Technical Analysis** | Can it be built & operated? | Technology assessment, plant layout |
| 3 | **Financial Analysis** | Will it be profitable? | NPV, IRR, Payback, PI |
| 4 | **Economic Analysis** | Will it benefit the nation? | Shadow prices, Social NPV |
| 5 | **Managerial Analysis** | Can the team execute? | Organization structure, HR review |
| 6 | **Social Cost-Benefit Analysis** | Is it socially desirable? | Employment generation, SCBA |
| 7 | **Environmental Analysis** | Is it ecologically safe? | EIA, carbon footprint |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **exam-critical formulas**. Memorize the variables, units, and the decision rule for each.

| Method | Formula | Decision Rule | Unit |
|--------|---------|---------------|------|
| **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | Accept if $NPV \ge 0$ | ₹ (Currency) |
| **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} - C_0 = 0$ | Accept if $IRR \ge r$ (cost of capital) | % (Percent) |
| **Payback Period (PBP)** | $PBP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ (simple) | Accept if $PBP \le$ target period | Years |
| **Discounted Payback Period** | Time for $\sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ to recover $C_0$ | Accept if within target | Years |
| **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** | $BCR = \dfrac{\sum \dfrac{B_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}}$ | Accept if $BCR \ge 1$ | Ratio (unitless) |
| **Profitability Index (PI)** | $PI = \dfrac{\sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}}{C_0}$ | Accept if $PI \ge 1$ | Ratio (unitless) |

**Where:**
- $CF_t$ = Net Cash Flow in year *t*
- $C_0$ = Initial Investment (at time 0)
- $r$ = Discount rate / Cost of capital
- $n$ = Project life in years
- $B_t$ = Benefits in year *t*
- $C_t$ = Costs in year *t*

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Never use the vertical pipe symbol $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table. The formula sheet above is structured safely.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In engineering entrepreneurship, project evaluation is used for:
- **Selecting R&D projects** (which technology to commercialize?)
- **Capacity expansion decisions** (add a new production line or not?)
- **Public infrastructure** (bridges, metro rail, water supply schemes)
- **Venture Capital funding decisions** (VCs always demand a DCF/NPV model)
- **Bank loan appraisal** (Banks in India use the *Tandon Committee* norms and *Chore Committee* norms for industrial project financing)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Net Present Value (NPV) from First Principles

### Step 1: Time Value of Money Concept
A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow, because today's rupee can be invested to earn a return **r**.

If you invest ₹1 today at rate *r* for **1 year**, the future value is:

$$FV = PV \times (1 + r)$$

### Step 2: Inverting to Find Present Value
Rearranging, the present value of ₹1 received after 1 year is:

$$PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1 + r)}$$

### Step 3: Extending to n Years
The present value of ₹1 received after *n* years is:

$$PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1 + r)^n}$$

This factor $\dfrac{1}{(1+r)^n}$ is called the **Discount Factor (DF)**.

### Step 4: Summing All Cash Flows
A project generates cash flows $CF_1, CF_2, \ldots, CF_n$ over its life, and requires an initial investment $C_0$ at time 0.

The present value of all future inflows is:

$$
PV_{\text{inflows}} \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t}
$$

### Step 5: NPV = PV of Inflows − Initial Investment
Subtracting the initial outlay gives the **Net Present Value**:

$$
NPV \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t} \;-\; C_0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:**
> - $NPV > 0$ → Project earns more than the discount rate → **Accept**
> - $NPV = 0$ → Project earns exactly the discount rate → **Indifferent**
> - $NPV < 0$ → Project destroys value → **Reject**

## 3.2 Worked Example (Typical 7-Mark KTU Sub-Question)

**Problem:** A project requires an initial investment of ₹5,00,000 and generates cash inflows of ₹1,50,000, ₹2,00,000, ₹2,50,000, and ₹2,00,000 at the end of years 1, 2, 3, and 4 respectively. The cost of capital is **10% p.a.** Calculate NPV and advise.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step A — Compute Discount Factors (DF):**
For r = 10%:
- $DF_1 = \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^1} = 0.9091$
- $DF_2 = \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^2} = 0.8264$
- $DF_3 = \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^3} = 0.7513$
- $DF_4 = \dfrac{1}{(1.10)^4} = 0.6830$

**Step B — Compute Present Value of Each Cash Flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1 &= 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.9091 = 1{,}36{,}365 \\
PV_2 &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8264 = 1{,}65{,}280 \\
PV_3 &= 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.7513 = 1{,}87{,}825 \\
PV_4 &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6830 = 1{,}36{,}600
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Sum the Present Values (Total PV of Inflows):**

$$
\text{Total PV} = 1{,}36{,}365 + 1{,}65{,}280 + 1{,}87{,}825 + 1{,}36{,}600
$$

$$
\text{Total PV} = 6{,}26{,}070
$$

**Step D — Subtract Initial Investment:**

$$
NPV = 6{,}26{,}070 - 5{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}26{,}070
$$

**Step E — Decision:**

Since $NPV = +\,₹1{,}26{,}070 > 0$, the project should be **ACCEPTED**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Evaluation Methods

| Criterion | NPV | IRR | Payback | BCR | PI |
|-----------|-----|-----|---------|-----|-----|
| Considers Time Value of Money? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (Simple) / ✅ (Discounted) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Considers Entire Life? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Gives Absolute Profit? | ✅ Yes (in ₹) | ❌ No (in %) | ❌ No (in years) | ❌ No (ratio) | ❌ No (ratio) |
| Useful for Mutually Exclusive Projects? | ✅ Best | ⚠️ Can mislead | ⚠️ Rough | ⚠️ Sometimes | ✅ Good |
| Complexity | Medium | High (trial & error) | Low | Medium | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule of Thumb:** When two mutually exclusive projects have **conflicting NPV and IRR rankings**, the **NPV method is preferred** because NPV measures *absolute wealth creation* while IRR measures *percentage return*.

## 3.4 Derivation of Payback Period Formula

For **uniform annual cash flows (A)** and initial investment $C_0$:

$$
\text{Payback Period} = \dfrac{C_0}{A}
$$

For **non-uniform cash flows**, payback is the year in which the cumulative cash flow first becomes non-negative (i.e., recovers $C_0$).

**Example:** If $C_0$ = ₹10 Lakhs and cumulative cash flows are:
- End of Year 1: ₹3 L
- End of Year 2: ₹6 L
- End of Year 3: ₹10 L
- End of Year 4: ₹13 L

Then Payback = **3 years exactly** (cumulative equals initial investment in year 3).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Project Evaluation Process — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Project Idea] --> B[Market Analysis]
    A --> C[Technical Analysis]
    A --> D[Managerial Analysis]
    B --> E[Financial Analysis]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Economic Analysis]
    E --> G[Social Cost Benefit Analysis]
    E --> H[Environmental Impact Analysis]
    F --> I[Risk and Sensitivity Analysis]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Decision Gate}
    J -->|NPV greater than 0| K[ACCEPT PROJECT]
    J -->|NPV less than 0| L[REJECT PROJECT]
    J -->|NPV equals 0| M[Indifferent Marginal Project]
    K --> N[Implementation Phase]
    N --> O[Interim Evaluation]
    O --> P[Ex Post Evaluation]
    P --> Q[Feedback to Future Projects]
```

## 4.2 Three Stages of Project Evaluation (Temporal Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ex Ante Evaluation] -->|Before Investment| B[Interim Evaluation]
    B -->|During Execution| C[Ex Post Evaluation]
    C -->|After Completion| D[Lessons Learned Loop]
    D --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrow from **D → A** represents the **feedback loop** — a hallmark of continuous learning in entrepreneurial project management.

## 4.3 Decision Logic for Capital Budgeting Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Evaluation] --> B{Is Project Life Defined?}
    B -->|No| C[Use Simple Payback]
    B -->|Yes| D{Is Cash Flow Uniform?}
    D -->|No| E[Use NPV and IRR]
    D -->|Yes| F{Is Discount Rate Known?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Compute NPV]
    F -->|No| H[Compute IRR and BCR]
    G --> I{NPV greater than 0?}
    H --> J{IRR greater than Cost of Capital?}
    I -->|Yes| K[Accept]
    I -->|No| L[Reject]
    J -->|Yes| K
    J -->|No| L
```

## 4.4 Inter-relationship of Evaluation Dimensions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Evaluation Framework] --> B[Technical Feasibility]
    A --> C[Financial Viability]
    A --> D[Economic Worth]
    A --> E[Social Desirability]
    A --> F[Managerial Capability]
    B --> G[GO NO GO Decision]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Project Sanctioned and Funded]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define Project Evaluation. List any four methods used for evaluating a project.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1.5 marks):** Project Evaluation is the systematic process of assessing a proposed project on technical, financial, economic, social, and managerial parameters to determine its feasibility, viability, and overall desirability before investment decisions are made.

**Four Methods (1.5 marks):**
1. **Net Present Value (NPV)** method
2. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** method
3. **Payback Period (PBP)** method
4. **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** method

*(Any four relevant methods accepted — Profitability Index, Discounted Cash Flow, Sensitivity Analysis also valid.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between Ex-Ante and Ex-Post Project Evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Ex-Ante Evaluation | Ex-Post Evaluation |
|-------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Timing** | Before project is sanctioned | After project is completed |
| **Purpose** | To decide *whether* to undertake the project | To assess *actual vs planned* performance |
| **Also called** | Project Appraisal / Feasibility Study | Terminal Evaluation / Post-Completion Audit |
| **Data** | Based on estimates & forecasts | Based on actual operating data |
| **Decision** | Go / No-Go | Lessons learned for future |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: (a) Explain in detail the various components or dimensions of project evaluation.** (7 marks)
**(b) A company is evaluating two mutually exclusive projects, A and B, each requiring an initial investment of ₹10 Lakhs. The cash inflows are:**

| Year | Project A (₹) | Project B (₹) |
|------|--------------|--------------|
| 1 | 3,00,000 | 5,00,000 |
| 2 | 4,00,000 | 4,00,000 |
| 3 | 5,00,000 | 3,00,000 |
| 4 | 4,00,000 | 2,00,000 |

**The cost of capital is 12% p.a. Calculate NPV of both projects using the discount factors 0.893, 0.797, 0.712, and 0.636. Advise which project should be selected.** (7 marks)

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Components of Project Evaluation (7 marks)

The seven components are:

1. **Market Analysis (1 mark):** Studies demand-supply gap, competition, target customers, pricing, distribution channels, and market share potential. Tools: Market survey, SWOT analysis, PESTEL.

2. **Technical Analysis (1 mark):** Examines technology choice, plant capacity, location, raw material availability, production process, and technical know-how. Includes layout, equipment selection, and utility requirements.

3. **Financial Analysis (1 mark):** Evaluates profitability using NPV, IRR, Payback, BCR, PI. Also covers cost of capital, working capital needs, and break-even analysis.

4. **Economic Analysis (1 mark):** Measures contribution to national income, balance of payments, and employment. Uses **shadow prices** and **social discount rate** instead of market prices.

5. **Managerial Analysis (1 mark):** Assesses the promoter's track record, organizational structure, management depth, and HR requirements.

6. **Social Cost-Benefit Analysis (1 mark):** Examines wider social impact — employment generation, regional development, displacement, health & safety.

7. **Environmental Impact Analysis (1 mark):** Studies pollution, waste disposal, ecological damage, compliance with environmental regulations, and sustainability.

#### Part (b) — NPV Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1: Project A — Discounted Cash Flows**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1^A &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.893 = 2{,}67{,}900 \\
PV_2^A &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.797 = 3{,}18{,}800 \\
PV_3^A &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.712 = 3{,}56{,}000 \\
PV_4^A &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.636 = 2{,}54{,}400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum of PVs (Project A):**

$$
\text{Total PV}_A = 2{,}67{,}900 + 3{,}18{,}800 + 3{,}56{,}000 + 2{,}54{,}400 = 11{,}97{,}100
$$

**NPV of Project A:**

$$
NPV_A = 11{,}97{,}100 - 10{,}00{,}000 = +\,₹1{,}97{,}100
$$

**Step 2: Project B — Discounted Cash Flows**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_1^B &= 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.893 = 4{,}46{,}500 \\
PV_2^B &= 4{,}00{,}000 \times 0.797 = 3{,}18{,}800 \\
PV_3^B &= 3{,}00{,}000 \times 0.712 = 2{,}13{,}600 \\
PV_4^B &= 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.636 = 1{,}27{,}200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum of PVs (Project B):**

$$
\text{Total PV}_B = 4{,}46{,}500 + 3{,}18{,}800 + 2{,}13{,}600 + 1{,}27{,}200 = 11{,}06{,}100
$$

**NPV of Project B:**

$$
NPV_B = 11{,}06{,}100 - 10{,}00{,}000 = +\,₹1{,}06{,}100
$$

**Step 3: Decision**

Since $NPV_A = +\,₹1{,}97{,}100 > NPV_B = +\,₹1{,}06{,}100 > 0$, **both projects are independently acceptable**, but since they are *mutually exclusive*, **Project A should be selected** because it generates a higher NPV (greater absolute wealth creation).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**Q: (a) What is Net Present Value? Explain its significance and limitations as a project evaluation tool.** (7 marks)
**(b) A project requires an initial investment of ₹8,00,000 and yields a uniform annual cash inflow of ₹2,50,000 for 5 years. Compute the Payback Period, NPV (at 10% discount rate, use PVIFA = 3.791), and Profitability Index. Advise on acceptance.** (7 marks)

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — NPV: Significance and Limitations (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):** NPV is the difference between the present value of cash inflows and the present value of cash outflows of a project, discounted at the cost of capital. Mathematically:

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0
$$

**Significance (3 marks):**
1. **Recognizes Time Value of Money** — accounts for the fact that money received later is worth less.
2. **Considers the Entire Project Life** — unlike Payback Period.
3. **Directly measures Wealth Creation** — NPV represents the actual rupee value added to the firm.
4. **Uses objective market-based discount rate** — making it consistent across projects.
5. **Helps in ranking** mutually exclusive projects objectively.
6. **Provides a clear Go/No-Go criterion:** Accept if NPV ≥ 0, Reject if NPV < 0.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Estimation Difficulty** — Requires accurate forecast of future cash flows and discount rate, both of which are uncertain.
2. **Not Comparable Across Scale** — A small project with high % return may be rejected over a large project with low % return when judged only by NPV (though usually NPV is preferred for scale).
3. **Ignores Project Size** when comparing with IRR (use PI to overcome this).
4. **Not useful for projects with non-quantifiable benefits** (e.g., social, environmental).

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $C_0 = ₹8{,}00{,}000$, $A = ₹2{,}50{,}000$, $n = 5$ years, $r = 10\%$, $PVIFA(10\%, 5) = 3.791$.

**Step 1: Payback Period (Simple, since uniform cash flow)**

$$
PBP = \dfrac{C_0}{A} = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{2{,}50{,}000} = 3.2 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 2: Net Present Value**

$$
PV_{\text{inflows}} = A \times PVIFA = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 3.791 = 9{,}47{,}750
$$

$$
NPV = 9{,}47{,}750 - 8{,}00{,}000 = +\,₹1{,}47{,}750
$$

**Step 3: Profitability Index**

$$
PI = \dfrac{PV_{\text{inflows}}}{C_0} = \dfrac{9{,}47{,}750}{8{,}00{,}000} = 1.18
$$

**Step 4: Decision**

- $NPV = +\,₹1{,}47{,}750 > 0$ → **Accept** ✅
- $PI = 1.18 > 1$ → **Accept** ✅
- $PBP = 3.2$ years (well within typical 5-year target) → **Accept** ✅

**All three criteria unanimously recommend ACCEPTANCE of the project.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Mixing up NPV and PVIFA tables** — confusing which annuity factor to use for given years.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract the initial investment** when computing NPV (only computing PV of inflows).
> 3. **Using simple Payback formula for non-uniform cash flows** — students apply $C_0/A$ blindly; for non-uniform cash flows, cumulative cash flow method is mandatory.
> 4. **Not stating the decision rule explicitly** — always write *"Since NPV > 0, the project is accepted"* — don't leave the examiner to infer.
> 5. **Ignoring units in the final answer** — ₹1,47,750 (not just "1,47,750").
> 6. **Confusing BCR with PI** — remember: BCR = PV(Benefits) / PV(Costs); PI = PV(Inflows) / Initial Investment.
> 7. **In mutually exclusive problems, picking the project with higher IRR instead of higher NPV** — KTU specifically tests this confusion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Summary for Last-Minute Reading:**

- ✅ **Project Evaluation = Multi-dimensional due diligence** (Technical + Financial + Economic + Social + Managerial + Environmental).
- ✅ **Three Time Stages: Ex-Ante (before), Interim (during), Ex-Post (after).**
- ✅ **NPV Formula:** $NPV = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$. **Decision:** Accept if NPV ≥ 0.
- ✅ **IRR Formula:** $NPV = 0$ solved for the rate. **Decision:** Accept if IRR ≥ cost of capital.
- ✅ **Payback Period:** Time required to recover initial investment from cash inflows. **Decision:** Accept if PBP ≤ target period.
- ✅ **BCR = PV(Benefits) / PV(Costs)**. **Decision:** Accept if BCR ≥ 1.
- ✅ **PI = PV(Inflows) / Initial Investment** = 1 + (NPV / Investment). **Decision:** Accept if PI ≥ 1.
- ✅ **For mutually exclusive projects → NPV is the supreme method** (not IRR, not Payback).
- ✅ **Discount Factor:** $DF_t = \dfrac{1}{(1+r)^t}$.
- ✅ **PVIFA (Present Value Interest Factor of Annuity):** $PVIFA(r, n) = \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$.
- ✅ **Always subtract the initial investment from the sum of discounted cash flows** to get NPV.
- ✅ **Project Evaluation ≠ Project Appraisal ≠ Project Feasibility Study** — these are related but distinct concepts.
- ✅ **UNIDO Guidelines** are the international standard reference for project evaluation in development finance.
- ✅ **In Indian context**, banks follow **Tandon Committee** and **Chore Committee** norms for industrial project appraisal.
- ✅ **Risk and Sensitivity Analysis** are integral to modern project evaluation — change one variable (e.g., discount rate) and see how NPV responds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Project Evaluation: Internal Rate of Return Method and Net Present Value Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT614 - ENTREPRENEURSHIP
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Methods of Project Evaluation: Internal Rate of Return Method and Net Present Value Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Net Present Value (NPV) Method

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT] **Net Present Value (NPV)** is the sum of the present values of all cash inflows and outflows associated with a project, discounted at the firm's cost of capital (also called the **hurdle rate** or **required rate of return**). Mathematically, it represents the *net wealth added* to the firm if the project is undertaken.

The decision rule is unambiguous:
- If **NPV > 0** $\Rightarrow$ **Accept** the project
- If **NPV < 0** $\Rightarrow$ **Reject** the project
- If **NPV = 0** $\Rightarrow$ The project is at the *indifference* point

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are considering buying a small coconut-selling stall on rent for **₹10,000**. The stall is expected to generate a net cash inflow of **₹3,000 per year for 5 years**. Before paying, you ask: *"If I instead deposited this ₹10,000 in a bank at 10% interest, how much would I earn?"* 

The bank's return is your **opportunity cost** (your cost of capital). You compare both: the *future stream of ₹3,000 discounted back to today* versus the *₹10,000 you must pay now*. If the discounted future earnings exceed ₹10,000, the stall is worth more than the bank deposit — **accept it**. That surplus, in today's rupees, is the **NPV**.

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight:** NPV converts uncertain future rupee values into *today's* equivalent rupee value, allowing apples-to-apples comparison between an outflow today and inflows spread over many years.

---

## 1.2 Internal Rate of Return (IRR) Method

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT] **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** is the discount rate at which the **Net Present Value of a project becomes exactly zero**. It is the *break-even* rate of return where the present value of cash inflows exactly equals the present value of cash outflows.

The decision rule:
- If **IRR > Cost of Capital (k)** $\Rightarrow$ **Accept** the project
- If **IRR < Cost of Capital (k)** $\Rightarrow$ **Reject** the project
- If **IRR = Cost of Capital (k)** $\Rightarrow$ **Indifference** point

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Going back to the coconut stall example — instead of being *told* the discount rate (10%), you ask: *"What is the **maximum** interest rate at which this stall would still be a worthwhile deal?"* 

You keep tweaking the discount rate in your calculation until the discounted inflows (₹3,000/yr for 5 years) *exactly match* your upfront investment of ₹10,000. That breakeven rate is the **IRR**. If your bank offers 10% but the stall gives an IRR of 15.24%, the stall *beats the bank* — accept it.

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight:** IRR is the project's *own* earning rate, independent of any external benchmark. The benchmark (cost of capital) is brought in *only at the decision stage* for comparison.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Notational Conventions (KTU 2024)

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $CF_0$ | Initial Investment (Cash Outflow at $t=0$) | Rupees (₹) |
| $CF_t$ | Net Cash Flow in year $t$ | Rupees (₹) |
| $k$ or $r$ | Cost of Capital / Discount Rate | **% per annum** |
| $n$ | Project Life | **Years** |
| $NPV$ | Net Present Value | Rupees (₹) |
| $IRR$ | Internal Rate of Return | **% per annum** |
| $PVA$ | Present Value Annuity Factor | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Both NPV and IRR are classified under **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)** techniques, distinct from non-DCF methods like Pay-Back Period and Accounting Rate of Return covered in earlier modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Time Value of Money

Both NPV and IRR rest on a single, fundamental principle:

> [!IMPORTANT] **A rupee today is worth more than a rupee tomorrow** — because today's rupee can be invested to earn a return.

This single idea gives rise to the **discounting** mechanism:

$$
PV \;=\; \frac{FV}{(1 + k)^n}
$$

Where:
- $PV$ = Present Value (today's worth)
- $FV$ = Future Value (worth at time $n$)
- $k$ = Discount (interest) rate
- $n$ = Number of periods

---

## 2.2 Net Present Value — Operational Logic

The project is decomposed into discrete cash flows $CF_0, CF_1, CF_2, \dots, CF_n$. Each future flow is discounted back to time zero and summed with the initial outflow.

**Why this works:** It quantifies the *net gain* (or loss) in *today's currency*. Because money has time value, the firm effectively asks: *"After compensating all capital providers at their required rate $k$, how much value is left over for shareholders?"*

### Step-by-Step Logic of NPV Evaluation

1. **Identify** the initial cash outflow at $t=0$ (purchase of machinery, setup costs, working capital).
2. **Forecast** after-tax operating cash inflows $CF_1, CF_2, \dots, CF_n$ for each year of the project's life.
3. **Determine** the cost of capital $k$ (often WACC — Weighted Average Cost of Capital).
4. **Discount** each $CF_t$ at rate $k$ to obtain its present value.
5. **Sum** all present values, including the negative $CF_0$.
6. **Decide** based on the sign of NPV.

### Advantages and Limitations of NPV

| Aspect | NPV Method |
| :--- | :--- |
| Considers Time Value of Money | **Yes** |
| Considers Entire Project Life | **Yes** |
| Considers Cost of Capital | **Yes (explicitly)** |
| Objective / Value-additive | **Yes (gold standard)** |
| Computationally intensive | Slightly (requires discount tables or calculator) |
| Sensitive to discount rate estimate | Yes — small change in $k$ can flip decision |

---

## 2.3 Internal Rate of Return — Operational Logic

The IRR is the discount rate $r$ that solves the **NPV = 0** equation:

$$
\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t} \;=\; 0
$$

This is a **polynomial equation of degree $n$ in $(1+r)$**, which generally has no closed-form algebraic solution. Hence IRR is found via:
- **Trial-and-error** (manual method)
- **Interpolation** between two adjacent discount rates
- **Financial calculator / spreadsheet functions** (e.g., Excel's `=IRR()`)

### Why IRR is Intuitive but Sometimes Misleading
The IRR answers: *"What is the project's intrinsic percentage return?"* This makes it appealing to non-financial managers and entrepreneurs. **However**, IRR assumes interim cash flows are reinvested at the IRR itself — an assumption that is often unrealistic. **NPV assumes reinvestment at the cost of capital $k$**, which is generally more defensible.

> [!WARNING] **Multiple IRR Problem:** For projects with *non-conventional* cash flows (e.g., negative cash flow in the middle years), the equation can yield **more than one IRR**, rendering the method ambiguous. NPV does not suffer from this defect.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Mandatory for Exam)

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Single Future Cash Flow PV | $PV = \dfrac{FV}{(1+k)^n}$ | Discounting one lump-sum future inflow |
| 2 | Uniform Series PV (Annuity) | $PVA = CF \times \dfrac{1 - (1+k)^{-n}}{k}$ | Discounting equal annual cash flows |
| 3 | Net Present Value (General) | $NPV = \displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+k)^t}$ | Master formula — applies to *any* cash flow pattern |
| 4 | NPV (with Annuity) | $NPV = -CF_0 + CF \times \dfrac{1 - (1+k)^{-n}}{k}$ | Equal annual inflows after initial investment |
| 5 | IRR Definition | $\displaystyle\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+IRR)^t} = 0$ | Set NPV $= 0$ and solve for $r$ |
| 6 | IRR by Interpolation | $IRR = R_L + \dfrac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (R_H - R_L)$ | When exact IRR is impractical; $L$ = lower rate, $H$ = higher rate |
| 7 | Profitability Index (PI) | $PI = \dfrac{\text{PV of Inflows}}{\text{PV of Outflows}} = 1 + \dfrac{NPV}{CF_0}$ | Companion metric to NPV for ranking |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Note for KTU Examiners:** In tables, the absolute value notation is rendered using $\vert \cdot \vert$ to keep the markdown table syntax intact. Always write `$\vert x \vert$` in LaTeX rather than `|x|`.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Entrepreneurship

- **Equipment Replacement Decisions:** A manufacturing engineer must decide whether to replace a 10-year-old CNC machine. NPV/IRR on the new machine's cash flows provides the answer.
- **R&D Project Selection:** A pharma company evaluating 5 candidate drugs — NPV ranks them; IRR benchmarks each against hurdle rate.
- **Renewable Energy Investments:** Solar/wind projects have 20–25 year horizons — NPV is essential because the bulk of returns come in later years.
- **Start-up Capital Rounds:** Entrepreneurs present DCF-based NPV/IRR projections to venture capitalists to justify valuations.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Annuity Present Value Factor

Starting from the general single-cash-flow discount formula:

$$
PV \;=\; \frac{FV}{(1+k)^n}
$$

A **uniform series** of cash flows $CF$ paid at the end of years $1, 2, \dots, n$ has a present value equal to the sum of each year's discount:

$$
PV_{\text{annuity}} \;=\; \frac{CF}{(1+k)^1} + \frac{CF}{(1+k)^2} + \cdots + \frac{CF}{(1+k)^n}
$$

$$
PV_{\text{annuity}} \;=\; CF \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+k)^t}
$$

This is a **finite geometric series** with first term $a = \frac{1}{1+k}$, common ratio $r = \frac{1}{1+k}$, and $n$ terms. Using the geometric series sum formula $S_n = a \cdot \dfrac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$:

$$
PV_{\text{annuity}} \;=\; CF \cdot \frac{1}{1+k} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\frac{1}{1+k}\right)^{n}}{1 - \frac{1}{1+k}}
$$

$$
PV_{\text{annuity}} \;=\; CF \cdot \frac{1}{1+k} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+k)^{-n}}{\frac{k}{1+k}}
$$

$$
PV_{\text{annuity}} \;=\; CF \cdot \frac{1 - (1+k)^{-n}}{k}
$$

This final closed-form expression is the **Present Value Annuity Factor (PVAF)**, multiplied by the constant cash flow $CF$:

$$
\boxed{\,PV_{\text{annuity}} \;=\; CF \times \left[\frac{1 - (1+k)^{-n}}{k}\right]\,}
$$

This factor is the heart of nearly every KTU numerical problem on project evaluation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the NPV Equation (with Initial Outflow)

For a project with an **initial investment** $CF_0$ at $t=0$ (already at present value — no discounting needed) followed by equal annual cash inflows $CF$ for $n$ years, NPV is:

$$
NPV \;=\; -CF_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{CF}{(1+k)^t}
$$

$$
NPV \;=\; -CF_0 + CF \cdot \frac{1 - (1+k)^{-n}}{k}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,NPV \;=\; -CF_0 + \left[CF \times PVAF(k, n)\right]\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the IRR Interpolation Formula

Let $R_L$ be a **lower** discount rate giving a *positive* NPV ($NPV_L > 0$), and $R_H$ be a slightly **higher** rate giving a *negative* NPV ($NPV_H < 0$). The true IRR lies between $R_L$ and $R_H$. Assuming NPV varies **linearly** over this small interval, we apply the *section formula* on a number line:

$$
\frac{IRR - R_L}{R_H - R_L} \;=\; \frac{NPV_L - 0}{NPV_L - NPV_H}
$$

Solving for $IRR$:

$$
\boxed{\,IRR \;=\; R_L + \frac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H} \times (R_H - R_L)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Reliability Note:** This interpolation is approximate. The accuracy improves as $R_H - R_L$ shrinks. A difference of 1–2 percentage points between $R_L$ and $R_H$ is standard KTU practice.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example (Model Numerical — KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A company is evaluating a machine that costs **₹50,000** to install. It will generate net cash inflows of **₹12,000 per year for 6 years**. The company's cost of capital is **10% p.a.** 
> **(a)** Compute the NPV and advise whether to accept.  
> **(b)** Compute the IRR using interpolation. Advise.

### Part (a): NPV Calculation

**Step 1:** Identify values.
- $CF_0 = 50{,}000$ (outflow)
- $CF = 12{,}000$ (annual inflow)
- $n = 6$ years
- $k = 10\% = 0.10$

**Step 2:** Compute the Present Value Annuity Factor at 10% for 6 years.

$$
PVAF(10\%, 6) \;=\; \frac{1 - (1.10)^{-6}}{0.10}
$$

Computing $(1.10)^{-6}$:

$$
(1.10)^1 = 1.10
$$

$$
(1.10)^2 = 1.21
$$

$$
(1.10)^3 = 1.331
$$

$$
(1.10)^4 = 1.4641
$$

$$
(1.10)^5 = 1.61051
$$

$$
(1.10)^6 = 1.771561
$$

Thus $(1.10)^{-6} = \frac{1}{1.771561} = 0.564474$.

**Step 3:** Substitute into the PVAF formula.

$$
PVAF(10\%, 6) \;=\; \frac{1 - 0.564474}{0.10} \;=\; \frac{0.435526}{0.10} \;=\; 4.35526
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the NPV equation.

$$
NPV \;=\; -50{,}000 + (12{,}000 \times 4.35526)
$$

$$
NPV \;=\; -50{,}000 + 52{,}263.12
$$

$$
\boxed{\,NPV \;=\; +2{,}263.12 \text{ (Rupees)}\,}
$$

**Decision:** Since **NPV > 0**, the project **should be accepted**. It adds ₹2,263.12 to the firm's wealth.

---

### Part (b): IRR Calculation by Interpolation

**Step 1:** We seek a rate $r$ such that:

$$
-50{,}000 + 12{,}000 \times PVAF(r, 6) \;=\; 0
$$

$$
PVAF(r, 6) \;=\; \frac{50{,}000}{12{,}000} \;=\; 4.16667
$$

**Step 2:** Bracket the rate using PVAF tables.

| Discount Rate | PVAF (6 years) | NPV (₹) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 10% | 4.35526 | +2,263.12 (positive) |
| 11% | 4.23054 | ? |
| 12% | 4.11141 | ? |

At 10%, PVAF = 4.35526 > 4.16667 (so NPV > 0). We need a **higher** rate. Try 12%: PVAF = 4.11141 < 4.16667 (so NPV < 0). Hence IRR lies between **11% and 12%**.

Compute exact PVAF at 11%:

$$
(1.11)^6 = 1.870414
$$

$$
(1.11)^{-6} = 0.534641
$$

$$
PVAF(11\%, 6) = \frac{1 - 0.534641}{0.11} = \frac{0.465359}{0.11} = 4.23054
$$

**Step 3:** Compute NPV at both 11% and 12% to obtain $NPV_L$ and $NPV_H$.

At 11%:

$$
NPV_L = -50{,}000 + (12{,}000 \times 4.23054) = -50{,}000 + 50{,}766.48 = +766.48
$$

At 12%:

$$
NPV_H = -50{,}000 + (12{,}000 \times 4.11141) = -50{,}000 + 49{,}336.92 = -663.08
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the interpolation formula with $R_L = 11\%$, $R_H = 12\%$.

$$
IRR = 11 + \frac{766.48}{766.48 - (-663.08)} \times (12 - 11)
$$

$$
IRR = 11 + \frac{766.48}{1429.56} \times 1
$$

$$
IRR = 11 + 0.5362
$$

$$
\boxed{\,IRR \;\approx\; 11.54\%\,}
$$

**Decision:** Since **IRR (11.54%) > Cost of Capital (10%)**, the project is **acceptable**. This is *consistent* with the NPV result.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — NPV vs IRR

| Criterion | NPV Method | IRR Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Output | Absolute rupee value | Percentage rate |
| Decision benchmark | Sign of NPV | Compare IRR with $k$ |
| Assumes reinvestment at | Cost of capital $k$ | The IRR itself (often unrealistic) |
| Multiple solutions | **No** (unique) | **Yes** (for non-conventional cash flows) |
| Mutually exclusive projects | Reliable for ranking | Can give misleading rankings |
| Scale sensitivity | Independent of project size | Biased toward smaller projects |
| KTU Examiner Preference | **Gold standard — preferred in ESE** | Widely used; tie-breaker with NPV |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Project Evaluation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Identify Project Proposal] --> A1[Estimate Initial Investment CF0]
    A1 --> A2[Forecast Annual Net Cash Inflows CF1 to CFn]
    A2 --> A3[Determine Cost of Capital k using WACC]
    A3 --> A4[Apply DCF Techniques]
    A4 --> B1[Compute NPV using Discounted Cash Flow]
    A4 --> B2[Compute IRR by Trial and Interpolation]
    B1 --> C1{NPV Greater Than Zero}
    C1 -- Yes --> D1[Accept Project - Wealth Created]
    C1 -- No --> D2[Reject Project - Wealth Destroyed]
    B2 --> C2{IRR Greater Than Cost of Capital k}
    C2 -- Yes --> D1
    C2 -- No --> D2
    D1 --> E1[Implement and Monitor]
    D2 --> E2[Drop or Redesign Proposal]
    E1 --> E3[Post Audit - Compare Actual vs Forecast Cash Flows]
    E2 --> E3
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — NPV vs IRR Consistency Check

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start with Project Cash Flow Stream] --> S1[Step 1 Compute NPV at Cost of Capital k]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2 Solve for IRR by setting NPV equal to Zero]
    S2 --> S3{Conventional Cash Flow Pattern}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4[NPV and IRR Always Agree]
    S3 -- No --> S5[Non Conventional Pattern Possible Multiple IRR or Sign Change]
    S5 --> S6[Use NPV as the Tie Breaker]
    S4 --> S7[Accept if NPV greater than Zero AND IRR greater than k]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Final Decision - Accept or Reject]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[T equals 0]:::neg -->|CF0 Outflow 50000| T1[T equals 1]:::pos
    T1 -->|CF1 Inflow 12000| T2[T equals 2]:::pos
    T2 -->|CF2 Inflow 12000| T3[T equals 3]:::pos
    T3 -->|CF3 Inflow 12000| T4[T equals 4]:::pos
    T4 -->|CF4 Inflow 12000| T5[T equals 5]:::pos
    T5 -->|CF5 Inflow 12000| T6[T equals 6]:::pos
    classDef neg fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef pos fill:#27ae60,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the diagrams:** The dark block at $T=0$ represents the initial investment outflow (₹50,000). The green blocks represent equal annual cash inflows (₹12,000) at the end of years 1 through 6. This visualization maps directly to the NPV/IRR formulas in Section 3.
