# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Distinguish between Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR) as methods of project evaluation."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | NPV | IRR |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Discounted net surplus in rupee terms | Discount rate that makes NPV $= 0$ |
| Output | Absolute amount in ₹ | Percentage rate (%) |
| Benchmark | Sign of NPV (positive/negative) | Compare with cost of capital $k$ |
| Reinvestment assumption | At cost of capital $k$ | At the IRR itself |
| Multiple solutions | Unique, no ambiguity | Multiple IRRs possible for non-conventional cash flows |
| Reliability for mutually exclusive projects | **More reliable** | Can give misleading rankings |

> **Mark Distribution (KTU Key):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for at least three distinguishing points, 1 mark for stating the reinvestment assumption.

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"What is the significance of cost of capital in evaluating a project using NPV and IRR methods?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **cost of capital ($k$)** is the minimum required rate of return expected by the providers of long-term funds (equity, debt, preference share). It is the **hurdle rate** used to discount future cash flows.

1. In **NPV**, $k$ is the actual discount rate used. A project is accepted only if its NPV at $k$ is positive, i.e., it earns *more* than the cost of the funds invested. **(1 mark)**
2. In **IRR**, $k$ is the **benchmark** for comparison. The project is accepted only if IRR > $k$. **(1 mark)**
3. A correct estimate of $k$ is therefore critical — under-estimation accepts poor projects; over-estimation rejects good ones. Firms typically use the **Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)**. **(1 mark)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule (Reminder):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is internally divided into sub-parts (a) and (b), typically **7 marks each**. Students answer **ONE** of the two alternatives offered.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A firm is considering two alternative proposals — **Project X** and **Project Y** — each requiring an initial investment of **₹1,00,000**.

- **Project X** generates annual cash inflows of **₹30,000 for 5 years**.
- **Project Y** generates annual cash inflows of **₹22,000 for 6 years**.

The firm's cost of capital is **12% p.a.** The firm can undertake **only one** project (mutually exclusive).

**(a)** Compute the NPV of both projects and recommend which one to accept. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compute the IRR of Project Y using the interpolation method and verify consistency with the NPV decision. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B-A

#### Part (a) — NPV of Both Projects (7 marks)

**Project X: $CF_0 = 1{,}00{,}000$, $CF = 30{,}000$, $n = 5$, $k = 12\%$**

Compute $PVAF(12\%, 5)$:

$$
(1.12)^5 = 1.762342
$$

$$
(1.12)^{-5} = \frac{1}{1.762342} = 0.567427
$$

$$
PVAF(12\%, 5) = \frac{1 - 0.567427}{0.12} = \frac{0.432573}{0.12} = 3.604776
$$

Now NPV:

$$
NPV_X = -1{,}00{,}000 + (30{,}000 \times 3.604776)
$$

$$
NPV_X = -1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}08{,}143.28
$$

$$
\boxed{\,NPV_X = +8{,}143.28 \text{ (Rupees)}\,}
$$

**Project Y: $CF_0 = 1{,}00{,}000$, $CF = 22{,}000$, $n = 6$, $k = 12\%$**

Compute $PVAF(12\%, 6)$:

$$
(1.12)^6 = 1.973823
$$

$$
(1.12)^{-6} = \frac{1}{1.973823} = 0.506631
$$

$$
PVAF(12\%, 6) = \frac{1 - 0.506631}{0.12} = \frac{0.493369}{0.12} = 4.111407
$$

Now NPV:

$$
NPV_Y = -1{,}00{,}000 + (22{,}000 \times 4.111407)
$$

$$
NPV_Y = -1{,}00{,}000 + 90{,}450.96
$$

$$
\boxed{\,NPV_Y = -9{,}549.04 \text{ (Rupees)}\,}
$$

**Recommendation:** Since $NPV_X = +₹8{,}143.28 > 0$ but $NPV_Y = -₹9{,}549.04 < 0$, **Project X should be accepted** and Project Y should be rejected. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — IRR of Project Y (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Set NPV = 0 and solve:

$$
-1{,}00{,}000 + 22{,}000 \times PVAF(IRR, 6) = 0
$$

$$
PVAF(IRR, 6) = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{22{,}000} = 4.545455
$$

**Step 2:** Bracket the rate. Using standard PVAF tables for $n = 6$:

| Rate | $PVAF$ | NPV (₹) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 6% | 4.917324 | +18,121.13 (positive) |
| 7% | 4.766540 | +14,863.88 (positive) |
| 8% | 4.622880 | +11,703.36 (positive) |
| 9% | 4.485919 | +8,690.21 (positive) |
| 10% | 4.355261 | +5,815.74 (positive) |
| 11% | 4.230538 | +3,071.84 (positive) |
| 12% | 4.111407 | -9,549.04 (negative) ← |

The target $PVAF = 4.545455$ falls between **8%** and **9%**.

**Step 3:** Apply the interpolation formula with $R_L = 8\%$, $R_H = 9\%$, $NPV_L = +11{,}703.36$, $NPV_H = +8{,}690.21$.

$$
IRR_Y = 8\% + \frac{11{,}703.36}{11{,}703.36 - 8{,}690.21} \times (9 - 8)
$$

$$
IRR_Y = 8 + \frac{11{,}703.36}{3{,}013.15} \times 1
$$

$$
IRR_Y = 8 + 3.884
$$

$$
\boxed{\,IRR_Y \;\approx\; 8\% + 0.39\% \;=\; 8.39\%\,}
$$

**Step 4:** Verification. Since **$IRR_Y = 8.39\% < k = 12\%$**, Project Y **fails the IRR test**, consistent with the negative NPV. Both methods agree — **reject Project Y**. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A company plans to invest **₹5,00,000** in a project that will yield cash inflows of **₹1,50,000 in Year 1**, **₹1,80,000 in Year 2**, and **₹2,50,000 in Year 3**. The cost of capital is **14% p.a.** Scrap value at the end of Year 3 is **₹50,000**.

**(a)** Compute the NPV of the project and state whether it is acceptable. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the advantages and limitations of the NPV method. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B-B

#### Part (a) — NPV with Uneven Cash Flows and Scrap Value (7 marks)

The cash flows are:
- $CF_0 = -5{,}00{,}000$
- $CF_1 = 1{,}50{,}000$
- $CF_2 = 1{,}80{,}000$
- $CF_3 = 2{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 \text{ (scrap)} = 3{,}00{,}000$
- $k = 14\%$

**Step 1:** Discount each cash flow.

$$
PV_1 = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{(1.14)^1} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{1.14} = 1{,}31{,}578.95
$$

$$
PV_2 = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{(1.14)^2} = \frac{1{,}80{,}000}{1.2996} = 1{,}38{,}504.16
$$

$$
(1.14)^2 = 1.2996
$$

$$
PV_3 = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{(1.14)^3} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{1.481544} = 2{,}02{,}498.75
$$

$$
(1.14)^3 = 1.14 \times 1.2996 = 1.481544
$$

**Step 2:** Sum all present values.

$$
NPV = -5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}31{,}578.95 + 1{,}38{,}504.16 + 2{,}02{,}498.75
$$

$$
NPV = -5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}72{,}581.86
$$

$$
\boxed{\,NPV = -27{,}418.14 \text{ (Rupees)}\,}
$$

**Decision:** Since **NPV < 0**, the project **should be rejected** at 14% cost of capital. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Advantages and Limitations of NPV (7 marks)

**Advantages (4 marks):**

1. **Time Value of Money:** NPV discounts all future cash flows, recognizing that money has time value. **(1 mark)**
2. **Considers Entire Project Life:** It accounts for all cash flows over the project's full economic life, not just payback period. **(1 mark)**
3. **Uses Cost of Capital Explicitly:** The hurdle rate $k$ is the firm's objective opportunity cost, making the decision criterion objective. **(1 mark)**
4. **Wealth Maximization:** A positive NPV directly increases shareholders' wealth — aligns with the firm's primary financial objective. **(1 mark)**

**Limitations (3 marks):**

1. **Estimate Sensitivity:** The result is highly sensitive to estimates of future cash inflows and the discount rate $k$. A small change can flip the decision. **(1 mark)**
2. **Complexity:** Uneven cash flows require year-by-year discounting, which is computationally heavier than methods like pay-back. **(1 mark)**
3. **Not Useful for Comparing Projects of Unequal Scale:** NPV in absolute rupees favours larger projects; the **Profitability Index (PI)** or **NPV per unit of investment** may be needed as a supplementary metric. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign on $CF_0$:** Many students add ₹1,00,000 instead of subtracting it, inflating the NPV artificially. *Always* write $NPV = -CF_0 + (\text{discounted inflows})$.
> 2. **Rounding PVAF too early:** Compute $PVAF$ to **at least 4 decimal places** before multiplying. Premature rounding propagates and inflates error.
> 3. **Interpolation error — reversed $R_L$ and $R_H$:** $R_L$ must be the rate giving **positive** NPV (lower rate), and $R_H$ must give **negative** NPV (higher rate). Reversing them yields a negative IRR.
> 4. **Skipping the decision statement:** Always end with *"Therefore, the project is accepted / rejected."* Examiners allot 1 mark for an explicit recommendation.
> 5. **Conflating IRR and ROI:** IRR is the *internal* rate from cash flows, not the Accounting Rate of Return. Don't mix them.
> 6. **Ignoring scrap/salvage value:** In KTU problems, scrap value is *added to the final year's cash inflow*, not treated separately. Omitting it is a common 2-mark loss.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **NPV** = Present Value of Inflows − Present Value of Outflows. **Accept if NPV > 0.**
- **IRR** = Discount rate that makes NPV = 0. **Accept if IRR > Cost of Capital.**
- Both methods are **Discounted Cash Flow (DCF)** techniques — they respect the **time value of money**.
- The **cost of capital $k$** is the firm's *hurdle rate*; it is the WACC in practice.
- **Annuity formula** $PVAF = \dfrac{1 - (1+k)^{-n}}{k}$ is the single most important tool for KTU numericals.
- **Interpolation formula** $IRR = R_L + \dfrac{NPV_L}{NPV_L - NPV_H}(R_H - R_L)$ for IRR problems.
- NPV assumes reinvestment at $k$; IRR assumes reinvestment at IRR — the former is more realistic.
- NPV is the **gold standard** for project acceptance decisions in academic valuation; IRR is intuitive and widely used in industry.
- **Mutually exclusive projects:** Always use **NPV** for ranking, not IRR (scale and timing bias).
- **Non-conventional cash flows** (sign changes after Year 0) can cause **multiple IRRs** — a known IRR weakness.
- **Profitability Index (PI)** is a useful companion: $PI = \dfrac{PV_{\text{inflows}}}{PV_{\text{outflows}}}$. Accept if $PI > 1$.
- For KTU 2024 ESE answers: **show every calculation step**, **quote the formula** before substituting, and **end with a clear decision sentence**.
- **Sign convention:** Cash outflows are negative; cash inflows are positive. Maintain this throughout the calculation.
- **PVAF values** to memorize: at 10% for 5 years = 3.7908; at 12% for 5 years = 3.6048; at 14% for 3 years = 2.3216. (Round to 4 decimal places.)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Objectives and Functions of Management, Scientific Management, General and Strategic Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEVT614 - ENTREPRENEURSHIP > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Objectives and Functions of Management, Scientific Management, General and Strategic Management # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Management? **Management** is the process of **planning, organizing, leading, and controlling** the resources of an organization (human, financial, physical, and informational) to achieve **predetermined objectives** effectively and efficiently. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Standard Definition (2024 Scheme):** > *"Management is the art of getting things done through and with people in formally organized groups. It is the process of optimizing human, material, and financial resources for the attainment of organizational goals."* — Adapted from Harold Koontz & Heinz Weihrich - **Effectiveness** = Doing the **right things** (achieving goals) - **Efficiency** = Doing things **right** (minimum wastage of resources) $$ \text{Management Performance} = \text{Effectiveness} \times \text{Efficiency} $$ ## 1.2 Objectives of Management > [!NOTE] > Management operates on a **3-tier objective hierarchy** that KTU examiners frequently test: | Tier | Objective Type | Description | Time Horizon | |------|---------------|-------------|--------------| | 1 | **Organizational / Economic Objectives** | Survival, profit, growth, market share | Long-term | | 2 | **Human / Social Objectives** | Employee welfare, work-life balance, social responsibility | Medium-term | | 3 | **Personal Objectives** | Personal growth, recognition, fair compensation for employees | Short-term | ## 1.3 The 14 Functions of Management (KTU Favourite Topic) The **core functions of management** are universally recognized as the **POLC framework**: 1. **P**lanning 2. **O**rganizing 3. **L**eading (or Directing / Staffing) 4. **C**ontrolling A more detailed **14-function expanded list** (per modern KTU 2024 syllabus) includes: > Planning, Organizing, Staffing, Directing, Motivating, Controlling, Coordinating, Communicating, Decision-Making, Leading, Innovation & Creativity, Representing, Mentoring, and Financial Management. ## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Football Team Manager" ⚽ Imagine a **football club manager** (like Pep Guardiola): - **Planning** → Decides the season strategy, training schedule, and formation before the match. - **Organizing** → Assigns positions (defender, midfielder, striker) and allocates the budget for transfers. - **Staffing** → Recruits the right players and support staff. - **Leading/Directing** → Motivates the team during half-time and provides on-field instructions. - **Controlling** → Reviews match statistics (possession, shots) and substitutes underperforming players. > [!TIP] > **Key Takeaway:** A manager who **plans well but fails to control** loses matches. A manager who **controls but cannot motivate** has no team spirit. **All 4 functions are interdependent.** ## 1.5 The Three Branches of Management Theory The KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus explicitly groups management thinking into three branches: 1. **Scientific Management** (Taylor, Gantt, Gilbreths) — *Task-oriented, shop-floor level* 2. **General Management / Administrative Management** (Fayol, Urwick) — *Organization-wide principles* 3. **Strategic Management** (Chandler, Ansoff, Porter) — *Long-term, top-level competitive positioning* --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Planning — The Brain of Management **Planning** is the **primary function** that sets the roadmap for the organization. **Step-by-step logic:** 1. **Set Objectives** → Define SMART goals (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) 2. **Develop Premises** → Identify assumptions about the future environment 3. **Identify Alternatives** → Brainstorm possible courses of action 4. **Evaluate Alternatives** → Cost-benefit analysis 5. **Select the Best Alternative** → Choose optimal path 6. **Implement the Plan** → Convert decision into action 7. **Follow-up & Review** → Measure actual vs planned > [!NOTE] > **Types of Plans (KTU Favourite):** > - **Strategic Plans** → Long-term (5+ years), top management > - **Tactical Plans** → Medium-term (1–5 years), middle management > - **Operational Plans** → Short-term (< 1 year), lower management > - **Single-use Plans** → Programs, projects, budgets > - **Standing Plans** → Policies, procedures, rules, strategies ## 2.2 Organizing — The Skeleton Organizing involves **dividing work, grouping tasks, and assigning responsibility**. **Core Principles of Organizing:** - **Division of Work** (Specialization) - **Departmentation** (Functional, Divisional, Matrix, Network) - **Delegation of Authority** - **Span of Control** (Graicunas' formula) - **Unity of Command** (One boss per subordinate) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Graicunas' Span of Control Formula** (Often asked in 3-mark questions): > > $$ > C = n \left(\frac{2^n}{2} + n - 1\right) > $$ > > Where: > - $C$ = Total number of direct relationships a manager must handle > - $n$ = Number of subordinates > > **Example:** If a manager has **5 subordinates**, the relationships = $5 \cdot (2^5/2 + 5 - 1) = 5 \cdot (16 + 4) = 100$ relationships. **Ideal Span of Control:** Generally **6 to 8 subordinates** at top level, **8 to 15** at lower level. ## 2.3 Staffing — The Heartbeat Staffing = **Manpower planning → Recruitment → Selection → Placement → Training & Development → Performance Appraisal → Compensation → Promotion**. The KTU 2024 syllabus highlights **Kaizen** (continuous improvement) and **Training within Industry (TWI)** as Japanese adaptations of Scientific Management. ## 2.4 Directing & Leading — The Soul Directing involves **issuing orders, motivating, leading, communicating, and supervising** to inspire employees to achieve goals. **Principles of Directing:** - **Unity of Direction** - **Effective Communication** (7 C's: Clear, Concise, Concrete, Correct, Coherent, Complete, Courteous) - **Motivation** (Maslow, Herzberg, Vroom) - **Leadership Styles** (Autocratic, Democratic, Laissez-Faire, Transformational, Transactional) ## 2.5 Controlling — The Watchdog **Controlling** ensures actual performance matches planned performance. **Step-by-step Control Process:** 1. **Setting Performance Standards** 2. **Measuring Actual Performance** 3. **Comparing Actual vs Standard** 4. **Analyzing Deviations** (Variance Analysis) 5. **Taking Corrective Action** > [!TIP] > **Budgetary Control Equation** (Used in cost-related KTU problems): > > $$ > \text{Variance} = \text{Budgeted Cost} - \text{Actual Cost} > $$ > > - Positive variance = **Favourable** (saved money) > - Negative variance = **Adverse** (overspent) ## 2.6 The Three Branches — Deep Theoretical Analysis ### A. Scientific Management (Frederick Winslow Taylor — 1911) **Core Premise:** Replace "rule of thumb" with scientifically determined best methods. > [!NOTE] > **Taylor's 4 Principles of Scientific Management** (Board-Exam Favourite — 7-mark question): > > 1. **Science, not Rule of Thumb** → Replace intuitive methods with scientific study > 2. **Harmony, not Discord** → Cooperation between management & workers > 3. **Cooperation, not Individualism** → Teamwork over personal gain > 4. **Development of Each Person** → Train workers to reach maximum efficiency **Techniques:** - **Time Study** (stopwatch method) — Gilbreth - **Motion Study** — Gilbreth (Therbligs — 18 micro-movements) - **Method Study** - **Fatigue Study** - **Differential Piece Wage System** — Pay higher rate for higher output - **Mental Revolution** — Mutual trust & shared prosperity ### B. General / Administrative Management (Henri Fayol — 1916) **Core Premise:** Develop universal principles applicable to ALL organizations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fayol's 14 Principles of Management** (MUST memorise for 14-mark question): > > 1. Division of Work > 2. Authority & Responsibility > 3. Discipline > 4. Unity of Command > 5. Unity of Direction > 6. Subordination of Individual Interest > 7. Remuneration > 8. Centralization > 9. Scalar Chain (Line of Authority) > 10. Order > 11. Equity > 12. Stability of Tenure > 13. Initiative > 14. Esprit de Corps (Team Spirit) **Fayol's 6 Activities of an Industrial Undertaking:** $$ \text{Total Operations} = \underbrace{T}_{\text{Technical}} + \underbrace{C}_{\text{Commercial}} + \underbrace{F}_{\text{Financial}} + \underbrace{S}_{\text{Security}} + \underbrace{A}_{\text{Accounting}} + \underbrace{M}_{\text{Managerial}} $$ ### C. Strategic Management (Modern — 1960s onwards) **Definition:** The set of managerial decisions and actions that determine the long-run performance of an organization. > [!NOTE] > **Strategic Management Process (5 stages):** > > 1. **Define the Mission & Vision** → Why we exist > 2. **Environmental Scanning** → SWOT, PESTEL, Porter's Five Forces > 3. **Strategy Formulation** → Corporate, Business, Functional level > 4. **Strategy Implementation** → Resource allocation, structure, culture > 5. **Strategy Evaluation & Control** → Performance metrics, corrective action **Michael Porter's Generic Strategies (KTU Favourite):** | Strategy Type | Description | Risk | |---------------|-------------|------| | **Cost Leadership** | Lowest cost producer | Price wars, imitation | | **Differentiation** | Unique value perception | Premium pricing, brand erosion | | **Focus** | Niche market specialization | Limited scope | ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Full Module) | Concept | Key Term | Inventor / Source | KTU Weightage | |---------|----------|-------------------|---------------| | Scientific Management | 4 Principles, Time Study, Motion Study, Differential Piece Wage | F.W. Taylor, F. & L. Gilbreth | 7 marks | | General Management | 14 Principles, 6 Activities | Henri Fayol | 7 marks | | Strategic Management | SWOT, Porter's 5 Forces, Generic Strategies | Chandler, Ansoff, Porter | 5 marks | | Functions of Management | POLC + 14 expanded | Koontz, Weihrich | 5 marks | | Span of Control | Graicunas' Formula $C = n(2^{n-1}+n-1)$ | V.A. Graicunas | 3 marks | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Taylor (Shop-floor / Worker level)** with **Fayol (Top / Manager level)**. Remember: **Taylor = Worker perspective; Fayol = Manager perspective.** ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Scientific Management** → Used in **manufacturing process optimization, lean production, Six Sigma, ISO certifications**. - **General Management Principles** → Universal applicability in **construction project management, IT service delivery, hospital administration**. - **Strategic Management** → Used by **engineering consultancies, tech startups (e.g., Zomato, Tesla)** for competitive positioning, R&D investment, and global expansion. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Detailed Walkthroughs > [!NOTE] > This section uses **analytical reasoning matrices** (since this is a management topic, mathematical derivations are replaced with conceptual step-by-step analyses of Taylor, Fayol, and Strategic Management frameworks). ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Management Schools | Dimension | Scientific Management | General Management | Strategic Management | |-----------|----------------------|--------------------|---------------------| | **Focus** | Task & worker efficiency | Universal principles for managers | Long-term competitive advantage | | **Level of Application** | Shop floor / Operational | Whole organization | Top / Corporate | | **Father / Originator** | F.W. Taylor (1911) | Henri Fayol (1916) | Alfred Chandler (1962) | | **Approach** | Bottom-up (worker → manager) | Top-down (manager → worker) | Outside-in (market → firm) | | **Key Tool** | Time & Motion Study | 14 Principles | SWOT, Porter's 5 Forces | | **Orientation** | Productivity-centric | Structure-centric | Market-centric | | **Time Horizon** | Daily / Weekly | Medium-term | 3–10 years | | **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High (Yearly) | Very High (Yearly) | High | ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis — Implementing Taylor's Scientific Management **Scenario:** A KTU-engineer-turned-entrepreneur wants to optimize a small assembly unit producing 100 mobile covers/day. ### Step 1: Observe & Identify Inefficiencies - Workers using varied methods → Inconsistent output - Wastage of raw material: **15%** - Average output: **80 units/day** vs target **120 units/day** ### Step 2: Scientific Method Study - Break the assembly task into **elemental motions** (Therbligs: reach, grasp, move, position, release) - Identify **18 micro-movements** per unit (Gilbreth's Therbligs) - Eliminate **unnecessary motions** (e.g., searching for tools) ### Step 3: Time Study - Use **stopwatch** to time each micro-motion - Calculate **standard time per unit** = 6 minutes (vs current 8 minutes) ### Step 4: Design the "One Best Way" - Standardize the optimal method - Document it as a Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) ### Step 5: Differential Piece Wage System $$ \text{Wage} = \begin{cases} \text{Rs. } 50/\text{unit} & \text{if output} \geq 120 \text{ units (high rate)} \\ \text{Rs. } 35/\text{unit} & \text{if output} < 120 \text{ units (low rate)} \end{cases} $$ ### Step 6: Mental Revolution - Train workers on the new SOP - Build mutual trust — workers share efficiency gains, management shares profits - Expected output rises from **80 → 120+ units/day** (50% productivity gain) > [!TIP] > **Exam Trick:** When asked to "Explain with an example", always use a **manufacturing/engineering scenario** — examiners relate better and award more marks. ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Analysis — Applying Fayol's 14 Principles in an Engineering Startup **Scenario:** A B.Tech graduate launches an IoT startup with 8 engineers. | # | Fayol's Principle | Application in Startup | |---|-------------------|------------------------| | 1 | **Division of Work** | 2 in hardware, 3 in software, 2 in sales, 1 in finance | | 2 | **Authority & Responsibility** | CEO holds authority + responsibility for profit/loss | | 3 | **Discipline** | Code of conduct, attendance policy, Agile stand-up rules | | 4 | **Unity of Command** | Each engineer reports to ONE team lead (not multiple) | | 5 | **Unity of Direction** | All hardware team works toward "Smart Meter v2" goal | | 6 | **Subordination of Individual Interest** | Personal projects deferred during client deadline | | 7 | **Remuneration** | Fixed + ESOPs (Employee Stock Ownership Plan) | | 8 | **Centralization** | Initial centralized; later delegated as team grows | | 9 | **Scalar Chain** | CEO → CTO → Team Lead → Engineer | | 10 | **Order** | Right person in right role (skill-based deployment) | | 11 | **Equity** | Fair treatment, no gender/caste bias in hiring | | 12 | **Stability of Tenure** | Retain talent with long-term contracts | | 13 | **Initiative** | Engineers encouraged to propose new product features | | 14 | **Esprit de Corps** | Team lunches, hackathons, cross-functional projects | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** Whenever a question asks "Apply Fayol's principles", present them in a **table format** like above. It is **scanning-friendly** and examiners award full marks (7/7). ## 3.4 Step-by-Step Analysis — Strategic Management Case Study **Scenario:** Zoho Corporation (Indian SaaS company) entering the European CRM market. ### Step 1: Mission & Vision - **Vision:** "Be the operating system for businesses worldwide" - **Mission:** Provide affordable, privacy-focused business software ### Step 2: Environmental Scanning (PESTEL + SWOT) | PESTEL Factor | Impact | |---------------|--------| | **P**olitical | GDPR data regulations — opportunity (Zoho's privacy-first model) | | **E**conomic | Eurozone recession — pressure to lower prices | | **S**ocial | Demand for remote-work tools — OPPORTUNITY | | **T**echnological | Cloud + AI boom — OPPORTUNITY | | **E**nvironmental | Green IT initiatives — opportunity | | **L**egal | GDPR compliance — Threat (high compliance cost) | **SWOT Snapshot:** | | Helpful | Harmful | |---|---------|---------| | **Internal** | Strengths: Privacy-first, low cost, multilingual | Weaknesses: Limited brand awareness in EU | | **External** | Opportunities: GDPR-driven shift from US players | Threats: Salesforce, HubSpot dominance | ### Step 3: Strategy Formulation (Porter's Generic Strategy) **Chosen Strategy: Differentiation (Privacy + Affordability)** $$ \text{Strategic Position} = \text{Cost Differentiation} \left(\frac{\text{Unique Value}}{\text{Lower Price}}\right) $$ ### Step 4: Implementation - Set up data centers in Frankfurt (GDPR compliance) - Launch 7-language support (vs Salesforce's English-first) - Hire local marketing teams in Germany, France ### Step 5: Evaluation - KPI: **New customer acquisition rate, churn rate, NPS score** - Review quarterly; pivot if ROI < 15% > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always end a strategic management answer with a **feedback loop** — show that the strategy is dynamic, not static. Examiners reward this. ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Management Approach | If your problem is... | Use this school | |-----------------------|-----------------| | Worker productivity is low on the shop floor | **Scientific Management** | | Organization-wide structure is chaotic | **General Management (Fayol)** | | Company is losing market share to competitors | **Strategic Management** | | Employee morale is low across departments | **Behavioral / Human Relations Approach** | | Innovation is stagnant | **Modern / Knowledge Management** | --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The POLC Framework — Functions of Management ```mermaid graph TD A[PLANNING
Set objectives, strategies, plans] --> B[ORGANIZING
Structure, resources, authority] B --> C[STAFFING
Recruit, train, develop people] C --> D[DIRECTING / LEADING
Motivate, communicate, lead] D --> E[CONTROLLING
Monitor, compare, correct] E -.Feedback Loop.-> A A -.Continuous Improvement.-> F[Organizational Goals Achieved] B -.Continuous Improvement.-> F C -.Continuous Improvement.-> F D -.Continuous Improvement.-> F E -.Continuous Improvement.-> F style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000 style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000 style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` ## 4.2 Taylor's Scientific Management — Detailed Architecture ```mermaid graph TD A[Shop Floor Inefficiency Identified] --> B[Time Study
Gilbreth's Stopwatch] A --> C[Motion Study
18 Therbligs] B --> D[Determine ONE BEST WAY] C --> D D --> E[Standardize SOP] E --> F[Train Workers] F --> G[Apply Differential Piece Wage System] G --> H[Monitor Output] H --> I{Output >= Target?} I -->|Yes| J[High Wage Paid
Mental Revolution Achieved] I -->|No| K[Re-train / Refine Method] K --> D J --> L[Increased Productivity and Profitability] style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style F fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000 style L fill:#FF6347,stroke:#FFF ``` ## 4.3 Strategic Management Process — 5-Stage Cyclical Model ```mermaid graph LR SM1[Stage 1
VISION and MISSION
Why we exist] --> SM2[Stage 2
ENVIRONMENTAL SCANNING
SWOT, PESTEL, Porter 5F] SM2 --> SM3[Stage 3
STRATEGY FORMULATION
Corporate, Business, Functional] SM3 --> SM4[Stage 4
STRATEGY IMPLEMENTATION
Resources, Structure, Culture] SM4 --> SM5[Stage 5
EVALUATION and CONTROL
KPIs, Balanced Scorecard] SM5 -.Feedback and Learning.-> SM1 SM5 -.Strategic Renewal.-> SM2 style SM1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style SM2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style SM3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style SM4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style SM5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Levels of Management — Block-Level Architecture ```mermaid graph TD TOP[Top Management
Board of Directors, CEO, MD] --> MID[Middle Management
General Managers, Department Heads] MID --> LOW[Lower Management / Supervisory
Foremen, Team Leads, Supervisors] LOW --> WORK[Workforce / Operational Staff] TOP -.Strategic Decisions.-> OUT1[Long-term Plans, Policies] MID -.Tactical Decisions.-> OUT2[Departmental Plans, Coordination] LOW -.Operational Decisions.-> OUT3[Daily Tasks, Direct Supervision] WORK -.Execution.-> OUT4[Production, Service Delivery] style TOP fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style MID fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style LOW fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style WORK fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.5 Comparative Mapping — Taylor vs Fayol vs Strategic Management ```mermaid graph TD MGMT[Management Theory Evolution] --> SC[Scientific Management
Taylor 1911
Shop Floor] MGMT --> GN[General Management
Fayol 1916
Organization-wide] MGMT --> ST[Strategic Management
Chandler 1962
Market-wide] SC --> SC1[4 Principles] SC --> SC2[Time and Motion Study] SC --> SC3[Differential Piece Wage] GN --> GN1[14 Principles] GN --> GN2[6 Activities TCAFSM] GN --> GN3[Scalar Chain] ST --> ST1[SWOT PESTEL] ST --> ST2[Porters 5 Forces] ST --> ST3[Generic Strategies] style MGMT fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style SC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style GN fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style ST fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.6 Porter's Five Forces — Competitive Pressure Model ```mermaid graph TD CTR[Competitive Rivalry
Industry Competitors] --> PROF[Industry Profitability] NEW[Threat of New Entrants] --> PROF SUB[Threat of Substitutes] --> PROF BUY[Bargaining Power of Buyers] --> PROF SUP[Bargaining Power of Suppliers] --> PROF style CTR fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style NEW fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000 style SUB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style BUY fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000 style SUP fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000 style PROF fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000 ``` --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answers) ### **Q1.** Define management. State any **two** objectives of management. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Management is the process of planning, organizing, staffing, directing, and controlling organizational resources to achieve predefined goals effectively and efficiently. As defined by **F.W. Taylor**, *"Management is knowing exactly what you want men to do and then seeing that they do it in the best and cheapest way."* **Two Objectives of Management:** 1. **Organizational Objectives** — Ensure survival, profitability, and growth of the enterprise. Example: Achieving a 20% year-on-year revenue increase. 2. **Human/Social Objectives** — Ensure employee welfare, fair wages, and a healthy work environment. Example: Implementing flexible work hours and mental wellness programs. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's note:** A definition + 2 clearly explained objectives fetches full 3 marks. Avoid writing 4 objectives — pick 2 with strong examples. --- ### **Q2.** Distinguish between **Effectiveness** and **Efficiency** in management. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Effectiveness | Efficiency | |-----------|--------------|------------| | **Meaning** | Doing the **right things** | Doing things the **right way** | | **Focus** | Goal achievement | Resource optimization | | **Measurement** | Output vs target | Output per unit input | | **Question it answers** | *"Are we doing the right task?"* | *"Are we doing the task in the optimal way?"* | | **Example** | A startup launching the right product (cricket bat for vegan cricketers) | Producing 1000 bats with minimum wood wastage | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic:** **"Effective = End (goal met) ; Efficient = Economic (low cost)"** --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice) ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` #### (a) Explain **Taylor's Principles of Scientific Management** in detail with its techniques. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** Frederick Winslow Taylor (1856–1915), known as the **"Father of Scientific Management"**, published his landmark work *The Principles of Scientific Management* in 1911. He observed that workers were using **"rule of thumb"** methods, leading to inefficiency. He proposed replacing these with **scientifically determined, standardized methods**. **The 4 Principles of Scientific Management:** 1. **Science, Not Rule of Thumb** - Traditional decision-making based on intuition and experience is replaced by **scientific method study**. - Example: Earlier a worker might hammer 20 nails/minute based on personal style; Taylor would scientifically determine the **optimal hammer weight, swing angle, and frequency** to maximize output. 2. **Harmony, Not Discord** - There must be **complete harmony** between workers and management. - Earlier, workers and managers viewed each other as adversaries. Taylor emphasized a **shared goal** — prosperity for both. 3. **Cooperation, Not Individualism** - Promote **teamwork** over individual glory. - In a factory, the foreman and workers should jointly find the best method, not compete. 4. **Development of Each Person to His Greatest Efficiency and Prosperity** - Train each worker scientifically to reach their **maximum potential**. - The "first-class worker" should be placed in scientifically selected jobs. **Techniques of Scientific Management:** 1. **Time Study** (Taylor & Gilbreth) — Using a stopwatch to time each micro-element of a task to set standard times. 2. **Motion Study** (F. & L. Gilbreth) — Identifying and eliminating wasteful motions. The Gilbreths classified work into **18 Therbligs** (basic micro-movements) — e.g., *grasp, move, position, release*. 3. **Method Study** — Finding the one best way of performing a task. 4. **Fatigue Study** — Designing work-rest cycles to reduce worker fatigue. 5. **Standardization** of tools, equipment, and working conditions. 6. **Differential Piece Wage System** — Paying a higher rate for output above standard; lower rate for output below standard. 7. **Mental Revolution** — A shift in mindset where workers and management share profits and responsibilities. **Conclusion:** Taylor's Scientific Management revolutionized the 20th-century factory floor and laid the foundation for **Lean Manufacturing, Six Sigma, and ISO 9001** quality systems used in modern engineering enterprises. --- #### (b) Explain **Henri Fayol's 14 Principles of Management** and comment on their **relevance in a modern engineering startup**. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** Henri Fayol (1841–1925), a French mining engineer, is known as the **"Father of General Management Theory"**. In his 1916 book *Administration Industrielle et Générale*, he identified **14 universal principles** applicable to all types of organizations. **The 14 Principles of Fayol:** 1. **Division of Work** — Specialization increases efficiency. 2. **Authority & Responsibility** — Authority is the right to give orders; responsibility is the obligation to perform. 3. **Discipline** — Employees must respect rules and agreements. 4. **Unity of Command** — Each employee receives orders from **only one superior**. 5. **Unity of Direction** — Activities with the same objective must have **one plan and one head**. 6. **Subordination of Individual Interest to General Interest** — Organizational goals supersede personal goals. 7. **Remuneration** — Fair compensation motivates employees. 8. **Centralization** — The degree to which authority is concentrated or dispersed. 9. **Scalar Chain** — The line of authority from top to bottom (Gang Plank exception allowed for emergencies). 10. **Order** — "A place for everything and everything in its place" — both material and social order. 11. **Equity** — Treat employees with fairness and kindness. 12. **Stability of Tenure** — High employee turnover is inefficient; retain talent. 13. **Initiative** — Encourage employees to develop and implement plans. 14. **Esprit de Corps** — "Unity is strength" — promote team spirit. **Application in a Modern Engineering Startup (Example: IoT-based Smart Home company):** | Principle | Application | |-----------|-------------| | Division of Work | 3 in firmware, 2 in mobile app, 1 in marketing | | Unity of Command | All firmware developers report to one CTO, not multiple project leads | | Order | Right engineer in right domain (RF expert in hardware, not sales) | | Initiative | Engineers free to propose new features; best idea gets funded | | Esprit de Corps | Weekly hackathons, pizza Fridays, cross-functional sprints | **Conclusion:** Although Fayol's principles were formulated over a century ago, they remain **highly relevant** in modern engineering organizations, especially startups where clarity of roles, fair compensation, and team spirit are critical for survival. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### (a) What is **Strategic Management**? Explain the **strategic management process** with reference to a real-world engineering company. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Strategic Management is the **continuous process** of planning, implementing, and evaluating **cross-functional decisions** that enable an organization to achieve its long-term objectives. It is the art and science of formulating, implementing, and evaluating **cross-functional strategies**. > *"Strategic management is the set of managerial decisions and actions that determine the long-run performance of a corporation."* — A.A. Thompson & A.J. Strickland **The 5-Stage Strategic Management Process:** 1. **Defining Vision, Mission, and Objectives** - **Vision** → The future aspiration (e.g., Tesla: "To accelerate the world's transition to sustainable energy") - **Mission** → The present purpose (e.g., Tesla: "Design, manufacture, and sell the world's best electric vehicles") - **Objectives** → SMART goals (e.g., 50% reduction in battery cost by 2030) 2. **Environmental Scanning (External & Internal Analysis)** - **External:** PESTEL (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal) + Porter's Five Forces - **Internal:** Value Chain Analysis, Resource-Based View, VRIO Framework - **Tools:** SWOT Matrix (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats) 3. **Strategy Formulation** - **Corporate-level:** Diversification, Vertical Integration, Mergers & Acquisitions - **Business-level:** Porter's Generic Strategies (Cost Leadership, Differentiation, Focus) - **Functional-level:** Marketing, Finance, HR, R&D strategies aligned to business strategy 4. **Strategy Implementation** - Allocate resources, design organizational structure, build culture, develop policies - Tesla built **Gigafactories** to vertically integrate battery production 5. **Strategy Evaluation & Control** - Use **Balanced Scorecard** (Financial, Customer, Internal Process, Learning & Growth perspectives) - Tools: KPIs, Benchmarking, Variance Analysis, Strategic Audit **Real-World Case: Tata Consultancy Services (TCS)** - **Vision:** "To be the global leader in IT services and consulting." - **SWOT:** Strength = scale; Weakness = wage inflation; Opportunity = AI/Cloud; Threat = US visa restrictions. - **Strategy:** Differentiation in **AI-led digital transformation** services. - **Implementation:** Acquired smaller AI firms, retrained 5 lakh+ employees. - **Control:** Tracked **digital revenue share** (target: 50% by 2030). **Conclusion:** Strategic Management is a **dynamic, cyclical, and learning-oriented** process. Modern engineering companies that fail to adopt strategic management (e.g., Blackberry, Nokia) lose market relevance. --- #### (b) Differentiate between **Scientific Management, General Management, and Strategic Management** in a tabular form. State the contribution of any **two pioneers** of management thought. (7 marks) **Model Answer:** **Comparative Table:** | Dimension | Scientific Management | General Management | Strategic Management | |-----------|----------------------|--------------------|---------------------| | **Father** | F.W. Taylor | Henri Fayol | Alfred D. Chandler | | **Year** | 1911 | 1916 | 1962 (*Strategy and Structure*) | | **Level** | Shop floor / Operational | Whole organization | Corporate / Top | | **Focus** | Worker & task efficiency | Managerial principles | Competitive positioning | | **Approach** | Bottom-up | Top-down | Outside-in | | **Key Tool** | Time Study, Motion Study | 14 Principles | SWOT, Porter's 5 Forces | | **Key Word** | Productivity | Structure | Competition | | **Time Horizon** | Short-term | Medium-term | Long-term (5–10 years) | **Contributions of Two Pioneers:** 1. **Frederick Winslow Taylor (1856–1915)** - Father of Scientific Management - Contributions: (i) Time Study (ii) Differential Piece Wage System (iii) Functional Foremanship (iv) 4 Principles of Scientific Management - Impact: Increased factory productivity by 200–300% in early 20th-century USA 2. **Henri Fayol (1841–1925)** - Father of General Management - Contributions: (i) 14 Principles of Management (ii) 6 Activities of an Industrial Undertaking (TCAFSM: Technical, Commercial, Financial, Security, Accounting, Managerial) (iii) Gang Plank concept - Impact: Provided the **first comprehensive management theory** applicable to all organizations — still taught in KTU 2024 syllabus **Honourable Mentions:** Mary Parker Follett (Human Relations), Max Weber (Bureaucracy), Peter Drucker (Management by Objectives), Michael Porter (Competitive Strategy). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Students 3–7 Marks Every Year:** > > 1. **Confusing Taylor with Fayol** — Taylor is for **workers** (task-level); Fayol is for **managers** (organization-level). Examiners deduct 2 marks instantly. > 2. **Memorizing 14 principles but not explaining** — Fayol's principles require a **one-line application example** for each. Listing without explanation = **2/7 marks** only. > 3. **Skipping diagrams in Strategic Management** — A **SWOT matrix or Porter's 5 Forces diagram** is mandatory for full marks. Examiners award 1–2 marks extra for visual aids. > 4. **Writing "Strategic Management" answer without a real company example** — Always cite **TCS, Infosys, Tesla, Zoho, or a KTU-recognized Indian startup**. Generic answers = 4/7 marks. > 5. **Forgetting the "Mental Revolution"** in Taylor's answer — This is a **favourite 1-mark bonus question** in KTU exams. > 6. **Missing the feedback loop** in Strategic Management — Show the **cycle from Evaluation back to Vision**. Static flowcharts lose marks. > 7. **Spelling errors** — "Fayol" is **not "Fayol's Principle" with the apostrophe-s in casual usage** in titles; use **"Fayol's 14 Principles"** correctly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️ > [!TIP] > **Final 5-Minute Revision Checklist (Save this for last-night revision!)** - **Management** = POLC (Plan, Organize, Lead/Motivate, Control) + Staffing + Coordination. - **Three Branches:** Scientific (Taylor, 1911), General (Fayol, 1916), Strategic (Chandler, 1962). - **Taylor's 4 Principles:** Science, Harmony, Cooperation, Development of Person. - **Fayol's 14 Principles:** Division, Authority, Discipline, Unity of Command, Unity of Direction, Subordination, Remuneration, Centralization, Scalar Chain, Order, Equity, Stability, Initiative, Esprit de Corps. - **Fayol's 6 Activities:** **T**echnical, **C**ommercial, **F**inancial, **S**ecurity, **A**ccounting, **M**anagerial → **TCAFSM** mnemonic. - **Taylor's Techniques:** Time Study, Motion Study (Therbligs), Method Study, Fatigue Study, Differential Piece Wage, Standardization, Mental Revolution. - **Graicunas' Span Formula:** $C = n(2^{n-1} + n - 1)$ where $n$ = number of subordinates. - **Strategic Management Process:** Vision → Environment Scan → Strategy Formulation → Implementation → Evaluation (with feedback loop). - **Porter's Generic Strategies:** Cost Leadership, Differentiation, Focus. - **SWOT = Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats** (Internal vs External × Positive vs Negative). - **PESTEL = Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal.** - **Effectiveness = Right things; Efficiency = Doing things right.** - **Key Mnemonic for Functions:** **P**ut **O**range **L**emons **C**arefully → **P**lanning, **O**rganizing, **L**eading, **C**ontrolling. - **Key Mnemonic for Fayol:** **"DCEUUDCSRSC-OESI"** — memorize in chunks. - **Modern Linkages:** Taylor → Lean, Six Sigma; Fayol → Modern HR Policies; Strategic Mgmt → Corporate Governance, ESG. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Specific:** > - **Part A** = 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (Module-level short answers) > - **Part B** = 1 question × 14 marks (with internal choice) — usually tests **Taylor, Fayol, or Strategic Management** in full depth > - **CO Mapping:** This module primarily maps to **CO4: Understand the principles and functions of management in entrepreneurial ventures.** > - **Bloom's Level:** **L2 (Understand) → L3 (Apply) → L4 (Analyze)** — KTU 2024 emphasizes application-level questions. **Best of luck with your KTU B.Tech Entrepreneurship exam! 🎓**

topics.title: Introduction to Human Resource Management: Planning, Job Analysis, Training, Recruitment and Selection, Marketing and Organizational Dimension of Enterprises
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT614 - ENTREPRENEURSHIP
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Human Resource Management: Planning, Job Analysis, Training, Recruitment and Selection, Marketing and Organizational Dimension of Enterprises

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Human Resource Management (HRM) — The Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *Human Resource Management (HRM) is the strategic and coherent approach to the management of an organization's most valued assets — the people working there — who individually and collectively contribute to the achievement of the organization's strategic objectives.*

In the KTU Entrepreneurship framework, HRM is the **entrepreneur's tool** to convert a vision into a functioning enterprise by acquiring, developing, and retaining the right talent.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Cricket Team" Intuition

Imagine you are the **captain of a cricket team** (the entrepreneur). The stadium, kits, and bats (capital) are ready, but the **match is won or lost by the 11 players on the field** (the human resources).

- **Recruitment** = Selecting the squad (local trials + auctions).
- **Job Analysis** = Defining each player's role (opener, all-rounder, bowler).
- **Training** = Net practice sessions before the match.
- **HR Planning** = Deciding how many batsmen vs. bowlers you need for a specific pitch.
- **Selection** = Final pick from trials based on form and fitness.
- **Retention** = Making sure your star bowler doesn't join another team.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The 2024 scheme treats HRM as an *integrated entrepreneurial function*, not a back-office admin task. It directly impacts scalability, culture, and venture survival.

---

## 1.2 HR Planning (Human Resource Planning - HRP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *HR Planning is the process of forecasting the future human resource requirements of an organization and determining how the existing human resource base will meet these requirements.*

**Key activities in HRP:**
1. **Demand Forecasting** — How many people are needed?
2. **Supply Forecasting** — How many are available internally + externally?
3. **Gap Analysis** — Surplus or Shortage?
4. **Action Plan** — Hire, Retrain, Redeploy, or Retrench.

### Intuition: The "Restaurant Dinner Rush" Analogy
On a **Saturday night**, a restaurant needs 8 waiters; on a **Monday**, only 3. HRP is the manager's *crystal ball* — predicting the *right number* of waiters for *every* night, balancing *over-staffing* (wasted salaries) and *under-staffing* (angry customers).

---

## 1.3 Job Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Job Analysis is the systematic process of determining the duties, tasks, and responsibilities of a job, and the human attributes (skills, knowledge, abilities) required to perform it.*

It produces two key documents:
- **Job Description (JD)** — *What* the job entails.
- **Job Specification (JS)** — *Who* can do it.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Job Analysis is the **upstream** process — it feeds into *every* other HR activity (recruitment, selection, training, appraisal, compensation). If Job Analysis is wrong, the entire HR pipeline is corrupted.

---

## 1.4 Training & Development

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Training is a short-term, job-oriented educational process that involves the development of skills, knowledge, and attitudes required for a specific job. Development is a long-term, broader process focused on overall growth.*

| Aspect | Training | Development |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Current Job | Future Role |
| Duration | Short-term | Long-term |
| Objective | Specific Skills | General Competence |
| For | Operatives/Executives | Managers/Executives |

### Intuition: The "Gym vs. Self-Study" Analogy
- **Training** = A trainer showing you the *correct squat form* for a specific sport.
- **Development** = Reading books, building a *winning mindset* for an Olympic career.

---

## 1.5 Recruitment & Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recruitment** = The process of *attracting* a pool of *qualified candidates* for a job (a **positive** process — inviting applications).
>
> **Selection** = The process of *choosing* the *most suitable* candidate from that pool and *rejecting* the unsuitable ones (a **negative/filtering** process).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The single most-asked distinction in the KTU exam is: *Recruitment is positive and Selection is negative.* This line alone can fetch 2 marks.

---

## 1.6 Marketing & Organizational Dimension of an Enterprise

### Marketing Dimension
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *The marketing dimension refers to the strategic and operational framework an entrepreneur uses to identify, create, communicate, and deliver value to customers — encompassing the 4Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion).*

### Organizational Dimension
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *The organizational dimension refers to the structural, cultural, and operational design of the enterprise — defining hierarchy, communication channels, authority, and culture that enable the venture to function as a coherent system.*

### Intuition: The "Building" Analogy
- **Marketing Dimension** = The **front-facing shop window** and signboard — what customers *see and experience*.
- **Organizational Dimension** = The **internal structure** — load-bearing walls, electrical wiring, plumbing. Customers never see it, but if it fails, the building collapses.

Together, they form the **strategic architecture** of any enterprise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The HRM Architecture — A Systems View

HRM is not a linear checklist; it is a **closed-loop system** where outputs of one process feed into the next, and feedback flows back upward.

### Step-by-Step Logic Chain

1. **Step 1 — Mission & Strategy Formulation:** The entrepreneur sets business goals. *Why?* Because HR decisions must *align* with business direction.
2. **Step 2 — HR Planning:** Translate business goals into *headcount needs* (demand) and assess *available talent* (supply).
3. **Step 3 — Job Analysis:** Document *what work exists* and *what people are needed* to do it.
4. **Step 4 — Recruitment:** Build a *candidate pool* using internal/external sources.
5. **Step 5 — Selection:** Filter the pool using tests, interviews, and assessments.
6. **Step 6 — Training & Development:** Onboard and upskill the chosen candidates.
7. **Step 7 — Performance Appraisal:** Measure output vs. expectations.
8. **Step 8 — Compensation & Retention:** Reward, motivate, and retain.
9. **Step 9 — Feedback Loop:** Appraisals reveal new training needs → cycle restarts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** In a tech-startup context, the **CTO** (entrepreneur) follows this exact loop. Job Analysis defines the *Full-Stack Developer* role; Recruitment sources from LinkedIn; Selection uses coding tests; Training involves tech-stack upskilling; Appraisal uses OKRs (Objectives and Key Results).

---

## 2.2 Detailed Process Breakdown

### A. HR Planning — The Three Sub-Steps

| Sub-Step | Activity | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Demand Forecasting | Statistical/Mathematical estimation of future HR needs | Headcount requirement |
| Supply Forecasting | Audit of current talent + external labor market | Available talent pool |
| Gap Analysis | Compare Demand vs. Supply | Surplus / Shortage / Balanced |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** "If demand > supply → **Recruit** or **Train**. If supply > demand → **Redeploy** or **Retrench**."

### B. Job Analysis — The 4 Methods

| Method | Description | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|
| **Observation** | Watching workers do the job | Repetitive manual jobs |
| **Interview** | Talking to incumbent + supervisor | Knowledge-intensive jobs |
| **Questionnaire** | Structured form filled by employees | Large workforce |
| **Diary / Log** | Worker records daily tasks | Rare/irregular jobs |

### C. Training — The Training Cycle (Systematic Approach)

The training process follows a **4-stage cycle**:

1. **Identifying Training Needs** (TNA — Training Needs Analysis)
2. **Designing the Training Program** (Objectives, content, method)
3. **Implementation** (On-the-job or Off-the-job delivery)
4. **Evaluation** (Kirkpatrick's 4 levels: Reaction → Learning → Behavior → Results)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kirkpatrick's 4-Level Evaluation Model** (frequently tested in KTU):
> - **Level 1 — Reaction:** Did trainees *like* the program?
> - **Level 2 — Learning:** Did they *acquire* knowledge/skills?
> - **Level 3 — Behavior:** Did their *on-job behavior* change?
> - **Level 4 — Results:** Did business *results* improve (sales, quality)?

### D. Recruitment — Sources at a Glance

| Internal Sources | External Sources |
|---|---|
| Transfers | Campus Placements |
| Promotions | Walk-in Interviews |
| Re-employment of ex-employees | Employment Exchanges |
| Employee Referrals | Advertisements (Print/Digital) |
| | Recruitment Agencies |
| | E-Recruitment (LinkedIn, Naukri) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse *Recruitment* with *Selection*. Recruitment *attracts*; Selection *rejects*. This is the most-marked distinction.

### E. Selection — The Filtering Funnel

1. **Application Screening** (CV/Resume)
2. **Preliminary Interview**
3. **Selection Tests** (Aptitude, Personality, Psychometric)
4. **Employment Interview** (Structured/Unstructured/Stress/Group)
5. **Reference & Background Checks**
6. **Medical Examination**
7. **Final Selection & Appointment Letter**

### F. Marketing Dimension — The 4Ps + Extended 3Ps

| The 4Ps | The 7Ps (Service Marketing) |
|---|---|
| Product | Product |
| Price | Price |
| Place | Place |
| Promotion | Promotion |
| | **People** |
| | **Process** |
| | **Physical Evidence** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Notice the **linkage** — *People* and *Process* in marketing directly connect to **HRM** and **Organizational Design**. This is a deliberate KTU 2024 scheme integration.

### G. Organizational Dimension — Key Components

1. **Organizational Structure** — Hierarchy, span of control, centralization.
2. **Organizational Culture** — Values, beliefs, norms.
3. **Organizational Design** — Mechanistic vs. Organic.
4. **Communication Channels** — Formal & Informal.
5. **Authority & Responsibility** — Unity of command, delegation.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Metric Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **standard HR metrics** that KTU may ask in numerical or short-answer form. Memorize the formulas.

| Metric | Formula | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Turnover Rate** | $\text{Turnover \%} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Separations}}{\text{Average Headcount}} \times 100$ | Measure retention effectiveness |
| **Absenteeism Rate** | $\text{Absenteeism \%} = \dfrac{\text{Total Absent Days}}{\text{Total Working Days}} \times 100$ | Workforce discipline |
| **Yield Ratio** | $\text{Yield Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{No. Hired}}{\text{No. of Applicants}} \times 100$ | Recruitment efficiency |
| **Selection Ratio** | $\text{Selection Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{No. Selected}}{\text{No. of Applicants}}$ | Lower = More selective = Higher candidate quality |
| **Cost per Hire** | $\text{CPH} = \dfrac{\text{Total Recruitment Cost}}{\text{No. of Hires}}$ | Recruitment ROI |
| **Training Effectiveness** | $\text{Effectiveness} = \dfrac{\text{Post-Training Performance} - \text{Pre-Training Performance}}{\text{Max. Possible Improvement}} \times 100$ | Kirkpatrick Level 2/3 evaluation |
| **Demand-Supply Gap** | $\text{Gap} = \text{Demand} - \text{Supply}$ | HRP decision trigger |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Break Safeguard Applied:** The above table uses no `|` symbols (which break markdown tables). All absolute-value/bar notations in formulas are written in LaTeX `$\vert$` form where needed.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Field Utility

In **engineering startups and corporate R&D units**, these HR concepts are operationally applied as:

- **HRP** → Project-based headcount planning (e.g., "we need 5 FPGA engineers by Q3").
- **Job Analysis** → Defining the JD for a "Robotics Systems Engineer" role.
- **Recruitment** → Campus hiring from IITs/NITs for technical roles.
- **Selection** → Technical interviews, coding tests, design challenges.
- **Training** → Onboarding on proprietary tools, safety training, Six Sigma.
- **Marketing Dimension** → Positioning the engineering product/service in the market.
- **Organizational Dimension** → Designing flat/hierarchical structures suited to innovation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section emphasizes **analytical derivations** of HR metrics and the **logical flow** of HR processes — written exhaustively without skipping steps.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: From Raw HR Data to Actionable Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to demonstrate *computational clarity* in HR metrics. Below is an exhaustive worked derivation using standard formulas.

### Problem Setup
A mid-size engineering startup recorded the following data for the year 2024:
- Number of employees on 1st Jan 2024: **120**
- Number of employees on 31st Dec 2024: **180**
- Total employees who left during the year: **30**
- Total working days in the year: **300**
- Total absent days logged across all employees: **1,800**
- Total applicants received for 5 open positions: **2,500**
- Total candidates selected: **5**
- Total recruitment cost incurred: **₹5,00,000**

We must derive: (a) Average Headcount, (b) Turnover Rate, (c) Absenteeism Rate, (d) Yield Ratio, (e) Selection Ratio, (f) Cost per Hire.

---

### Step 1 — Average Headcount

The **Average Headcount** is the arithmetic mean of the opening and closing headcount, used as the denominator for rate calculations.

$$
\text{Average Headcount} = \dfrac{\text{Opening Headcount} + \text{Closing Headcount}}{2}
$$

$$
\text{Average Headcount} = \dfrac{120 + 180}{2} = \dfrac{300}{2} = 150
$$

**Interpretation:** On an average day in 2024, the company had **150 employees** on its rolls.

---

### Step 2 — Turnover Rate

$$
\text{Turnover \%} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Separations}}{\text{Average Headcount}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Turnover \%} = \dfrac{30}{150} \times 100 = 20\%
$$

**Interpretation:** **20% of the workforce left in 2024.** For an engineering startup, this is *high*; industry benchmark is 10–15%. Action: Investigate exit reasons, improve retention.

---

### Step 3 — Absenteeism Rate

Total possible working days = Average Headcount × Total Working Days.

$$
\text{Total Possible Working Days} = 150 \times 300 = 45{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Absenteeism \%} = \dfrac{\text{Total Absent Days}}{\text{Total Possible Working Days}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Absenteeism \%} = \dfrac{1{,}800}{45{,}000} \times 100 = 4.0\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Absenteeism of **4%** is *acceptable*; the safety benchmark in manufacturing is <3%, services <5%.

---

### Step 4 — Yield Ratio

Yield Ratio indicates the *efficiency* of converting applicants to hires.

$$
\text{Yield Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Hires}}{\text{No. of Applicants}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Yield Ratio} = \dfrac{5}{2{,}500} \times 100 = 0.2\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Only **0.2%** of applicants were hired. This is *very selective*, which is healthy for a high-skill engineering role — but it also implies a *very high* recruitment screening cost.

---

### Step 5 — Selection Ratio

$$
\text{Selection Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{No. Selected}}{\text{No. of Applicants}}
$$

$$
\text{Selection Ratio} = \dfrac{5}{2{,}500} = 0.002
$$

**Interpretation:** A selection ratio of **0.002** (1 hire for every 500 applicants) is highly competitive. Per KTU note: *Lower selection ratio = higher candidate quality (statistically).*

---

### Step 6 — Cost per Hire

$$
\text{CPH} = \dfrac{\text{Total Recruitment Cost}}{\text{No. of Hires}}
$$

$$
\text{CPH} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{5} = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}000
$$

**Interpretation:** Each hire cost the company **₹1,00,000**. This is a *benchmark* for budgeting next year's recruitment spend.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: What Action for What Gap?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU artifact** — it converts the demand-supply gap into a managerial action.

| Condition | Trigger Formula | Managerial Action | KTU Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Demand > Supply | Gap > 0 | Recruit externally / Train existing | Expand talent pipeline |
| Demand < Supply | Gap < 0 | Redeploy / Retrench / VRS | Optimize cost base |
| Demand = Supply | Gap = 0 | Retain + Engage | Stability phase |
| High Turnover | TR > 15% | Exit interview + compensation review | Retention strategy |
| Low Absenteeism | AR < 3% | Reward & recognize | Healthy culture |
| Low Yield Ratio | YR < 1% | Reduce applicant pool cost | Use targeted sourcing |

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Recruitment vs. Selection

| Parameter | Recruitment | Selection |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Attract applicants | Reject unsuitable |
| **Nature** | Positive | Negative |
| **Approach** | Simple | Complex |
| **Outcome** | Larger pool | Smaller, qualified pool |
| **Performed by** | HR + Line managers | Specialized selectors |
| **Time spent per candidate** | Low | High |
| **Result of failure** | Few applicants | Wrong hire → high cost |
| **KTU Mnemonic** | **"Invite In"** | **"Filter Out"** |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation: Training Effectiveness Index

Suppose pre-training test score (out of 100) = **45**, post-training score = **78**, maximum possible score = **100**.

$$
\text{TE} = \dfrac{\text{Post} - \text{Pre}}{\text{Max} - \text{Pre}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{TE} = \dfrac{78 - 45}{100 - 45} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{TE} = \dfrac{33}{55} \times 100 = 60.0\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Training delivered **60%** of the *achievable* improvement. Anything >50% is considered effective.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
