# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Short-Answer Conceptual Question
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**
> **"Define Break-Even Point. Explain the concept of Margin of Safety and its significance for an entrepreneur."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
The **Break-Even Point (BEP)** is the level of sales at which **Total Revenue equals Total Cost**, resulting in zero profit and zero loss. Mathematically:

$$
BEP_{(units)} = \dfrac{Fixed\ Cost}{Selling\ Price - Variable\ Cost\ per\ unit}
$$

The **Margin of Safety (MoS)** is the difference between **actual/anticipated sales and break-even sales**. It represents the amount by which sales can drop before the business incurs a loss. **Significance:** (i) Acts as a cushion against sales decline, (ii) Indicates the risk of loss, (iii) Helps entrepreneurs set sales targets, and (iv) A higher MoS% (typically > 25%) indicates a more financially resilient venture. *(Marks: 1 for BEP definition + formula, 1 for MoS definition + formula, 1 for significance.)*

---

### Question 2: Short-Answer Conceptual Question
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Style]**
> **"What is a Balance Sheet? List and briefly explain any four ratios used in balance sheet analysis."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
A **Balance Sheet** is a financial statement that summarizes a company's **Assets, Liabilities, and Shareholders' Equity** at a specific point in time, based on the fundamental equation: **Assets = Liabilities + Equity**.

**Four key ratios:**

1. **Current Ratio** $= \dfrac{\text{Current Assets}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ — Measures short-term liquidity (ideal: 2:1).

2. **Quick Ratio** $= \dfrac{\text{Current Assets} - \text{Inventory}}{\text{Current Liabilities}}$ — Strict test of liquidity (ideal: 1:1).

3. **Debt-Equity Ratio** $= \dfrac{\text{Long-term Debt}}{\text{Shareholders' Equity}}$ — Measures financial leverage (ideal: ≤ 1:1).

4. **Proprietary Ratio** $= \dfrac{\text{Shareholders' Equity}}{\text{Total Assets}}$ — Shows owner's contribution to assets (ideal: > 0.5).

*(Marks: 0.5 for definition, 0.5 each for 4 ratios × 4 = 2.0, 0.5 for ideal benchmarks.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Module Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Set A Style]**
> **(a)** Explain the **assumptions, limitations, and graphical representation** of Break-Even Analysis. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A company sells a product at **₹200 per unit**. The variable cost is **₹120 per unit**, and the total fixed costs are **₹4,00,000**. Calculate: *(i)* BEP in units, *(ii)* BEP in sales value, *(iii)* P/V Ratio, *(iv)* Profit when sales are 8,000 units, and *(v)* Sales units required to earn a target profit of **₹1,60,000**. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks)

**Assumptions of Break-Even Analysis:**
1. Costs can be accurately classified as **Fixed** or **Variable** (no semi-variable costs).
2. **Fixed costs remain constant** across the relevant range of output.
3. **Variable cost per unit is constant** — i.e., linear cost behavior.
4. **Selling price per unit is constant** — no discounts, no price variation with volume.
5. The firm produces and sells a **single product** (or maintains a constant sales mix).
6. **Production volume equals sales volume** — no change in inventory.
7. The analysis is for a **single time period** and ignores inflation, taxation, and time value of money.

**Limitations of Break-Even Analysis:**
1. **Unrealistic in multi-product firms** due to differing cost structures and sales mixes.
2. **Ignores semi-variable costs** like electricity (with fixed charges + variable usage).
3. **Static in nature** — fails to account for dynamic market changes, inflation, or competition.
4. **Assumes linear relationships** which may not hold in real life due to economies/diseconomies of scale.
5. **Over-simplifies the entrepreneur's reality** by ignoring qualitative factors like customer satisfaction, brand equity, and employee morale.

**Graphical Representation (Description):**
On a graph with **Output (units)** on the X-axis and **Cost/Revenue (₹)** on the Y-axis:
- The **Fixed Cost line** is horizontal (parallel to X-axis).
- The **Total Cost line** slopes upward, starting from the FC intercept.
- The **Total Revenue line** also slopes upward, starting from the origin.
- The **intersection of TR and TC** is the BEP.
- The region **left of BEP** = Loss Zone; **right of BEP** = Profit Zone.

*(Marks distribution: 3 for assumptions, 2 for limitations, 2 for graphical explanation.)*

---

### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Selling Price (P) = ₹200
- Variable Cost per unit (V) = ₹120
- Fixed Cost (FC) = ₹4,00,000

**Step 1: Compute Contribution Margin per unit.**

$$
CM = P - V = 200 - 120 = ₹80\ \text{per unit}
$$

**Step 2: Compute P/V Ratio.**

$$
P/V\ Ratio = \dfrac{CM}{P} = \dfrac{80}{200} = 0.40\ \text{or}\ 40\%
$$

**Part (i): BEP in units**

$$
BEP_{(Q)} = \dfrac{FC}{CM} = \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000}{80}
$$

$$
\boxed{BEP_{(Q)} = 5{,}000\ \text{units}}
$$

**Part (ii): BEP in sales value (₹)**

$$
BEP_{(₹)} = BEP_{(Q)} \times P = 5{,}000 \times 200
$$

$$
\boxed{BEP_{(₹)} = ₹10{,}00{,}000}
$$

**Part (iii): P/V Ratio** (already computed above)

$$
\boxed{P/V\ Ratio = 40\%}
$$

**Part (iv): Profit at sales of 8,000 units**

$$
\text{Total Contribution at 8,000 units} = 80 \times 8{,}000 = ₹6{,}40{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Profit} = \text{Total Contribution} - FC = 6{,}40{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Profit} = ₹2{,}40{,}000}
$$

**Part (v): Required units for target profit of ₹1,60,000**

$$
\text{Required Units} = \dfrac{FC + \text{Target Profit}}{CM} = \dfrac{4{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}60{,}000}{80} = \dfrac{5{,}60{,}000}{80}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Required Units} = 7{,}000\ \text{units}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Set B Style]**
> **(a)** Distinguish between **Fixed Costs and Variable Costs** with examples. Explain how the **P/V Ratio** and **Margin of Safety** are useful managerial decision-making tools for an entrepreneur. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** From the following Balance Sheet of **M/s GreenBuild Constructions Ltd.**, compute: *(i)* Current Ratio, *(ii)* Quick Ratio, *(iii)* Debt-Equity Ratio, *(iv)* Proprietary Ratio, and *(v)* Capital Gearing Ratio. **Interpret each ratio with reference to the standard benchmark.** *(7 Marks)*

#### Balance Sheet as on 31st March 2025

| **Liabilities** | **₹ (in 000s)** | **Assets** | **₹ (in 000s)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Equity Share Capital | 6,000 | Land & Building | 5,000 |
| 9% Preference Capital | 2,000 | Plant & Machinery | 4,000 |
| 11% Debentures | 4,000 | Furniture | 500 |
| Bank Overdraft | 1,000 | Inventory (Stock) | 3,500 |
| Sundry Creditors | 1,500 | Sundry Debtors | 1,800 |
| Bills Payable | 500 | Prepaid Expenses | 200 |
| Outstanding Salary | 200 | Cash at Bank | 800 |
| **Total** | **15,200** | **Total** | **15,200** |

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks)

**Distinction between Fixed and Variable Costs:**

| **Basis** | **Fixed Costs** | **Variable Costs** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Costs that remain constant irrespective of output level (within a relevant range) | Costs that vary in direct proportion to the level of output |
| **Behavior** | Do not change with production volume | Change in total with production volume |
| **Per Unit Behavior** | Per-unit fixed cost **decreases** as output increases | Per-unit variable cost **remains constant** |
| **Examples** | Rent, Salaries, Insurance, Depreciation, Property Tax | Raw materials, Direct labor (piece-rate), Sales commission, Packaging |
| **Control** | Time-based and managed periodically | Unit-based and managed daily |

**Usefulness of P/V Ratio:**
- The **P/V Ratio** (Contribution / Sales × 100) indicates the **contribution efficiency** of each rupee of sales.
- A **higher P/V ratio** implies that a greater portion of each sales rupee is available to cover fixed costs and generate profit.
- It helps the entrepreneur **compare product profitability** and **make product mix decisions** — products with higher P/V ratios are preferred when fixed costs are high.
- It is used to **compute BEP in sales value** and **target sales for desired profit**.

**Usefulness of Margin of Safety (MoS):**
- MoS = Actual Sales − BEP Sales.
- It represents the **cushion** against sales decline before the firm slips into losses.
- A **higher MoS%** (> 25%) indicates a **safer, more resilient business**; a low MoS% signals high risk.
- It assists in **forecasting, budgeting, and contingency planning**.
- It is a key metric in **risk assessment** by banks and venture capital investors.

*(Marks: 2 for distinction table, 2.5 for P/V Ratio, 2.5 for MoS.)*

---

### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Classification of Items**

**Current Assets (CA):**
- Inventory (Stock): ₹3,500
- Sundry Debtors: ₹1,800
- Prepaid Expenses: ₹200
- Cash at Bank: ₹800
- **Total CA = ₹6,300 (in 000s)**

**Current Liabilities (CL):**
- Bank Overdraft: ₹1,000
- Sundry Creditors: ₹1,500
- Bills Payable: ₹500
- Outstanding Salary: ₹200
- **Total CL = ₹3,200 (in 000s)**

**Long-term Debt:** 11% Debentures = ₹4,000 (in 000s)

**Shareholders' Equity (Net Worth):** Equity Share Capital = ₹6,000 (in 000s)

**Preference Capital:** ₹2,000 (in 000s)

**Total Assets (TA):** ₹15,200 (in 000s)

---

**Part (i): Current Ratio**

$$
\text{Current Ratio} = \dfrac{6{,}300}{3{,}200} = 1.97
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Current Ratio} \approx 1.97 : 1}
$$

**Interpretation:** Just below the standard 2:1 benchmark — the firm has **adequate but not abundant** short-term liquidity. No immediate alarm, but **slight improvement in working capital is recommended**.

---

**Part (ii): Quick Ratio**

$$
\text{Quick Ratio} = \dfrac{CA - \text{Inventory}}{CL} = \dfrac{6{,}300 - 3{,}500}{3{,}200} = \dfrac{2{,}800}{3{,}200} = 0.875
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Quick Ratio} = 0.875 : 1}
$$

**Interpretation:** Below the standard 1:1 benchmark — the firm **cannot fully meet immediate obligations** without selling inventory. **Action needed:** Improve cash position or reduce inventory holding.

---

**Part (iii): Debt-Equity Ratio**

$$
\text{Debt-Equity Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{LT Debt (Debentures)}}{\text{Equity}} = \dfrac{4{,}000}{6{,}000} = 0.67
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Debt-Equity Ratio} = 0.67 : 1}
$$

**Interpretation:** Below the standard 1:1 benchmark — the firm is **conservatively financed** with low financial risk. There is **scope to leverage further debt** for expansion if profitable opportunities exist.

---

**Part (iv): Proprietary Ratio**

$$
\text{Proprietary Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Equity}}{\text{Total Assets}} = \dfrac{6{,}000}{15{,}200} = 0.3947 = 39.47\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Proprietary Ratio} = 0.39 : 1\ (39.47\%)}
$$

**Interpretation:** Below the 50% ideal — owners finance only about 40% of total assets. The remaining 60% is funded through liabilities, indicating **moderate dependence on external funds**.

---

**Part (v): Capital Gearing Ratio**

$$
\text{Capital Gearing} = \dfrac{\text{Preference Capital} + \text{LT Debt}}{\text{Equity Share Capital} + \text{Reserves}} = \dfrac{2{,}000 + 4{,}000}{6{,}000} = \dfrac{6{,}000}{6{,}000} = 1.0
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Capital Gearing Ratio} = 1 : 1}
$$

**Interpretation:** The firm is **evenly geared** — fixed-return capital equals equity capital. A high gearing indicates **high fixed financial charges** and vulnerability to profit downturns.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Examinations:**
> 
> 1. **Formula Inversion Error (–2 marks):** Students frequently compute $\dfrac{V}{P - V}$ instead of $\dfrac{P - V}{P}$ for the P/V Ratio. Always cross-verify your formula.
> 
> 2. **Confusing "Sales" with "BEP Sales" (–1 mark):** Margin of Safety = **Actual Sales − BEP Sales**, NOT actual profit or contribution.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring "Inventory" in Quick Ratio (–1 mark):** Quick Ratio = $(CA - Inventory) / CL$. Forgetting to subtract inventory is a recurring mistake.
> 
> 4. **Mixing "Total Debt" and "Long-term Debt":** Debt-Equity ratio uses ONLY long-term debt. Including current liabilities is a frequent error.
> 
> 5. **No Interpretation (–2 to –3 marks):** KTU examiners deduct heavily when students compute ratios but **fail to interpret** them. Always state whether the ratio is *favorable, unfavorable, or borderline* with respect to the standard benchmark.
> 
> 6. **Unit Confusion in BEP:** Always specify whether the answer is in **units** or in **₹ (sales value)**. Failing to do so costs at least 0.5 marks.
> 
> 7. **No assumption stated in BEP theory (–1 mark):** When asked about BEA assumptions, simply listing them without contextualizing is incomplete. KTU expects brief, application-oriented understanding.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### Break-Even Analysis — Quick Recall
- ✅ BEP is the **no-profit, no-loss point** where TR = TC.
- ✅ Formula: $BEP_{(units)} = FC / (P - V)$.
- ✅ Formula: $BEP_{(₹)} = FC / (P/V\ Ratio)$.
- ✅ P/V Ratio = $(P - V) / P \times 100$.
- ✅ Margin of Safety = Actual Sales − BEP Sales; expressed in ₹ or as a percentage.
- ✅ Required units for target profit = $(FC + \text{Profit}) / (P - V)$.
- ✅ Assumptions: linear cost behavior, single product, constant prices, FC and VC well-defined.
- ✅ Limitations: unrealistic in multi-product settings, ignores time value, inflation, and semi-variable costs.

### Balance Sheet Analysis — Quick Recall
- ✅ Balance Sheet equation: **Assets = Liabilities + Equity**.
- ✅ **Current Ratio = CA / CL** (ideal 2:1).
- ✅ **Quick Ratio = (CA − Inventory) / CL** (ideal 1:1).
- ✅ **Cash Ratio = (Cash + Marketable Securities) / CL** (ideal ≥ 0.5:1).
- ✅ **Debt-Equity = LT Debt / Equity** (ideal ≤ 1:1).
- ✅ **Proprietary Ratio = Equity / Total Assets** (ideal ≥ 0.5).
- ✅ **Interest Coverage = EBIT / Interest** (ideal ≥ 3).
- ✅ **Capital Gearing = (Pref. Capital + LT Debt) / (Equity + Reserves)**.
- ✅ Always **interpret** the ratio with respect to its benchmark.

### Final KTU Exam Tips
- 📌 Memorize the **8–10 standard ratios** with formulas, ideal benchmarks, and one-line interpretations.
- 📌 In numericals, **show all working steps** — partial marking depends on visibility of methodology.
- 📌 For theory, use **tables and bullet points** for clarity; examiners love structured answers.
- 📌 When asked to **"compute and interpret,"** always give both — computation alone yields only 60–70% of the marks.
- 📌 Practice at least **3–4 numerical problems** on BEP and **2–3 balance sheet ratio problems** before the KTU end-semester exam.
- 📌 Understand the **logic behind the formulas** (e.g., why Quick Ratio excludes inventory) — KTU increasingly tests application, not just rote memorization.
